lessons.name: Intro
lessons.concept:
Alright, let's get this over with. You're here to learn Nim.
It's a compiled, statically typed language that looks like Python but has the performance of C.
This means you get speed, but you also have to actually think about your types.
No more sloppy dynamic-language nonsense.
lessons.task: There's no code to write. Just read the above and internalize it. Press Enter to continue.
lessons.cross_language_notes: This is an orientation lesson — other languages don't have a direct equivalent. It sets expectations for the Nim learning path.

docs.title: Tutorial (Part 1)
docs.summary: Nim's official beginner tutorial covering syntax, types, control flow, and I/O.

lessons.name: Hello World
lessons.concept:
Your first rite of passage. In Nim, the `echo` procedure prints stuff to the console.
It's simple, clean, and gets the job done. Don't overthink it.
lessons.task: Write a single line of Nim code that prints the string "Hello, Nim!".
lessons.solution: echo "Hello, Nim!"
lessons.hint: Just `echo` the exact string. With quotes. You can figure this out.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: print(). C: printf(). Rust: println!(). Nim's echo adds a newline automatically and is simpler than all of them.

docs.title: Tutorial - Hello World
docs.summary: Nim's official introduction: hello world, basic syntax, and your first program.

docs.title: Manual (Full)
docs.summary: Complete Nim language reference manual covering all features.

lessons.name: Compiling & Running
lessons.concept:
Nim is a compiled language. The command `nim c -r your_file.nim` does two things:
1. `c`: Compiles your Nim code into a C executable.
2. `-r` or `--run`: Runs the resulting executable immediately after compiling.
This is the workflow you'll use constantly. Get used to it.
lessons.task: Write a program that prints the number 42.
lessons.solution: echo 42
lessons.hint: Same as before. `echo` the number. No quotes this time, genius.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python runs via interpreter (python file.py). C needs explicit compile (gcc -o prog file.c && ./prog). Nim's nim c -r combines both steps.

docs.title: Nim Compiler User Guide
docs.summary: Compiler flags, optimization, cross-compilation, and build configuration.

docs.title: Tutorial - Compiling
docs.summary: How to compile and run Nim programs with `nim c -r`.

lessons.name: Comments
lessons.concept:
Comments are for you, not the compiler. Use them to explain *why*, not *what*.
Nim uses `#` for single-line comments and `#[ ... ]#` for multi-line comments.
They are ignored by the compiler entirely.
lessons.task: Add a single-line comment containing the text "This is my first Nim comment" to your code. The program should also print the number 7.
lessons.solution:
# This is my first Nim comment
echo 7
lessons.hint: Put a `#` at the start of the line for your comment, then `echo 7` on the next line.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python it's `#` too, but multi-line is `""" ... """`. In C++ single-line is `//` and multi-line is `/* ... */`. Nim uses `#[ ... ]#` for multi-line.

docs.title: Manual - Comments
docs.summary: Single-line (#) and multi-line (#[...]#) comment syntax.

lessons.name: Basic I/O
lessons.concept:
`echo` prints to stdout. `readLine()` reads a line from stdin.
These are your primary tools for interacting with the user.
`readLine()` from the `os` module blocks until the user presses Enter.
lessons.task: Import `os`. Use `writeFile` to create a file named "output.txt" containing the string "done". Then `echo` the contents of that file using `readFile`.
lessons.hint: `import os`. Use `writeFile("output.txt", "done")`. Then `echo readFile("output.txt")`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python uses `open(...).read()` and `open(...).write(...)`. Nim's `readFile`/`writeFile` are simpler top-level procs from the `os` module.

docs.summary: Nim's implicit prelude: built-in types, operators, and core procedures.

docs.title: std/os
docs.summary: Operating system interface: file operations, env vars, process info, paths.

docs.title: Tutorial - Input/Output
docs.summary: Reading from stdin and writing to stdout with echo, readLine, writeFile, readFile.

lessons.name: let vs var
lessons.concept:
Nim has two ways to declare variables: `let` and `var`.
- `let` creates an immutable variable. Once assigned, you can't change it. It's a constant.
- `var` creates a mutable variable. You can change its value whenever you want.
Rule of thumb: Use `let` by default. Mutability is a footgun. Only use `var` when you know you need it.
lessons.task: Declare an immutable string named `name` with the value "Nim" and a mutable integer `age` with the value 15. The program shouldn't print anything.
lessons.solution:
let name = "Nim"
var age = 15
lessons.hint: The syntax is `let name = "value"` and `var age = 15`. If it compiles, you did it right.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python has no immutability for simple variables. C++ has const. Rust has let (immutable by default) and let mut. Nim's let/var is closest to Rust's approach.

docs.title: Tutorial - Variables
docs.summary: Declaring variables with let and var, type inference, and basic types.

docs.title: Manual - Var Statement
docs.summary: Declaration and initialization of variables with let and var.

lessons.name: Basic Types
lessons.concept:
Nim is statically typed. You can, and often should, declare types explicitly.
The syntax is `var name: type = value`.
Basic types include: `int`, `string`, `float`, `bool`.
lessons.task: Declare four `var` variables, one of each basic type: an `int` named `i`, a `string` named `s`, a `float` named `f`, and a `bool` named `b`. Assign them any valid values. No output needed.
lessons.solution:
var i: int = 1
var s: string = "a"
var f: float = 1.0
var b: bool = true
lessons.hint: Example: `var i: int = 10`. Do that for all four types.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python is dynamically typed (no type declarations). C++ uses int, std::string, float, bool. Rust uses i32, String, f64, bool. Nim uses lowercase names like Python but is statically typed like C++/Rust.

docs.title: Tutorial - Basic Types
docs.summary: Nim's built-in types: int, float, string, bool, char, and their usage.

docs.title: Manual - Types
docs.summary: Complete reference for Nim's type system.

lessons.name: Type Inference
lessons.concept:
Explicitly writing types is good, but sometimes the compiler can figure it out on its own.
When you declare a variable with `let` or `var` and assign a value immediately, Nim infers the type.
`let name = "Nim"` is the same as `let name: string = "Nim"`.
`var age = 20` is the same as `var age: int = 20`.
It's clean, simple, and you should use it when the type is obvious.
lessons.task: Declare a mutable integer named `counter` with a value of 0 using type inference.
lessons.solution: var counter = 0
lessons.hint: Just use `var counter = 0`. The compiler knows it's an integer.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: always inferred (dynamic). C++17: auto. Rust: let/let mut with inference. Nim's type inference works for let/var declarations but not proc signatures.

docs.title: Manual - Type Inference
docs.summary: How the Nim compiler infers types from context.

lessons.name: Constants (const)
lessons.concept:
Beyond `let` (runtime immutable) and `var` (mutable), Nim has `const` for compile-time constants.
`const` values are evaluated at compile time, making them zero-cost.
Use `const` for true constants like math values, not for things you compute at runtime.
lessons.task: Declare a `const` named `GREETING` with the value "Hello". Also declare a `let` variable `message` that reassigns `GREETING` to the string `"Hello, Nim!"`. Echo `message`.
lessons.solution:
const GREETING = "Hello"
let message = GREETING & ", Nim!"
echo message
lessons.hint: `const GREETING = "Hello"`. Then `let message = GREETING & ", Nim!"`. Then `echo message`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: C++ has `constexpr`, Rust has `const`, Python has no true compile-time constants. Nim's `const` is closest to Rust's `const`.

docs.title: Manual - Const
docs.summary: Compile-time constant evaluation with the const keyword.

lessons.name: Type Conversions
lessons.concept:
Sometimes you need to convert between types. Nim is strict about this — no implicit conversions.
Use built-in conversion procs: `int()`, `float()`, `string()`, `$` (to string sugar).
`$` is especially useful: it converts any type that has a `$` operator to a string.
lessons.task: Declare `let x = 42` (an int). Convert it to a `float` and then to a `string` using `$`. Echo the result.
lessons.hint: Use `float(x)` to convert the int to a float. Then `$y` converts the float to a string for echo.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python auto-converts int→float on division. C++ has static_cast. Rust has `as` keyword. Nim uses proc-style conversions like `int()`, `float()`.

docs.title: Manual - Type Conversions
docs.summary: Explicit type conversion using int(), float(), $, and other converters.

lessons.name: Distinct Types
lessons.concept:
In Nim, `distinct` types allow you to create new types that are distinct from existing ones, providing a way to enforce type safety and clarity. For instance, you can define a `TemperatureCelsius` type that is distinct from a regular `float`, ensuring that you don't accidentally mix up temperatures in Celsius and Fahrenheit. Here's a quick example:

```nim
type
  TemperatureCelsius = distinct float

let temp: TemperatureCelsius = 25.0
```

In this snippet, `TemperatureCelsius` is a distinct type based on `float`, and you can only assign `float` values to it. This helps prevent bugs related to type mismatches.
lessons.task: Create a distinct type for a temperature in Celsius and use it in a simple function that converts it to Fahrenheit. The function should take a `TemperatureCelsius` as input and return the temperature in Fahrenheit as a `float`. The formula to convert Celsius to Fahrenheit is `F = C * 9/5 + 32`. Your function should be named `celsiusToFahrenheit`. After writing the function, test it with a `TemperatureCelsius` value of `25.0` and print the result. The expected output should be `77.0`.
lessons.solution:
import strformat

type
  TemperatureCelsius = distinct float

proc celsiusToFahrenheit(temp: TemperatureCelsius): float =
  return float(temp) * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0

let tempC = TemperatureCelsius(25.0)
let tempF = celsiusToFahrenheit(tempC)

echo fmt"The temperature in Fahrenheit is: {tempF}"  # Expected output: The temperature in Fahrenheit is: 77.0
lessons.hint: Remember, with `distinct` types, you need to explicitly convert between the distinct type and the base type using `float(temp)` or similar. Also, ensure your function is named `celsiusToFahrenheit` and takes a `TemperatureCelsius` as input.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, you might use a class or a named tuple to achieve similar type safety. In Rust, you can use the 'newtype' pattern with a struct. In C++, you can use a class with private members and public methods to enforce type safety.

docs.title: Manual - Distinct Types
docs.summary: Creating type-safe wrappers around existing types with distinct.

lessons.name: Subrange Types
lessons.concept: Subrange types in Nim are a way to restrict the values a variable can hold to a specific range. This is useful for ensuring that your program doesn't accidentally use invalid values. For example, if you have a variable that should only hold values between 0 and 5, you can define a subrange type like this: `type MySubrange = range[0..5]`. Now, any variable of type `MySubrange` can only hold values from 0 to 5. Trying to assign a value outside this range will result in a compile-time or runtime error. This is a simple yet powerful feature that helps catch bugs early in the development process.
lessons.task: Define a subrange type called `Age` that can only hold values from 0 to 120. Create a variable of type `Age`, assign it the value 30, and print `"myAge = " & $myAge`.
lessons.solution:
type
  Age = range[0..120]

var
  myAge: Age

myAge = 30
echo "myAge = ", myAge
lessons.hint: Remember, subrange types are like a strict club that only allows certain values. If you try to join with a value that's not allowed, you'll get kicked out at compile-time or runtime. So, make sure your value is within the specified range!
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, you can use the `typing` module to create a custom type with a limited range, but it doesn't enforce the range at runtime. In Rust, you can use the `newtype` pattern with bounds checking. In C++, you can use `std::enable_if` to restrict template parameters to a certain range, but it's more complex and less idiomatic than Nim's subrange types.

docs.title: Manual - Subrange Types
docs.summary: Restricting values to a specific range using range types.

lessons.name: Type Aliases
lessons.concept:
Type aliases in Nim are a way to give a new name to an existing type. This can make your code more readable and maintainable, especially when dealing with complex types. For example, you can create an alias for `int` called `IntAlias`. Here's how you do it:

```nim
type
  IntAlias = int
```

Now you can use `IntAlias` wherever you would use `int`. It's like giving a nickname to a type.

This feature is similar to `typedef` in C or `type` in Rust. It's a simple yet powerful tool in Nim's type system.
lessons.task: Create a type alias for an integer and use it in a variable declaration. Your code should compile and run without errors. The variable should be named `myNumber` and should hold the value `42`.
lessons.solution:
type
  IntAlias = int

var
  myNumber: IntAlias = 42

echo myNumber
lessons.hint: Remember, type aliases are just nicknames for types. You're not creating a new type, just giving an existing one a new name. So, `IntAlias` is just another way to say `int`. Use it like you would use `int`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: `IntAlias = int` / Rust equivalent: `type IntAlias = i32;` / C++ equivalent: `using IntAlias = int;`

docs.title: Manual - Type Aliases
docs.summary: Creating alternative names for existing types.

docs.title: Tutorial - Type Aliases
docs.summary: Creating aliases for existing types to improve readability.

lessons.name: Procedures (proc)
lessons.concept:
Procedures, or `proc`, are Nim's functions. They take arguments and can return a value.
The syntax is: `proc name(arg1: type, arg2: type): returntype =`
The body of the proc is indented. The return value is the last expression, or you can use `return`.
lessons.task: Write a procedure named `addNumbers` that takes two integers (`a` and `b`) and returns their sum. Then, call it with `5` and `10` and `echo` the result.
lessons.solution:
proc addNumbers(a: int, b: int): int = a + b
echo addNumbers(5, 10)
lessons.hint: Define the proc first. `proc addNumbers(a: int, b: int): int = a + b`. Then, on a new line, `echo addNumbers(5, 10)`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: def add(a, b): return a + b. C++: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }. Rust: fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 { a + b }. Nim's proc syntax is closest to Rust but uses = instead of { }.

docs.title: Tutorial - Procedures
docs.summary: Defining and calling procedures, parameters, return types, and recursion.

docs.title: Manual - Procedures
docs.summary: Full reference for proc declarations, parameters, return types, and calling conventions.

lessons.name: if/else/elif
lessons.concept:
Conditional logic. You've seen this before in other languages. It's not complicated.
`if condition:`
  `...`
`elif other_condition:`
  `...`
`else:`
  `...`
lessons.task:
Write a procedure `checkSign` that takes an integer.
- If the number is greater than 0, it should `echo` "Positive".
- If it's less than 0, it should `echo` "Negative".
- Otherwise, it should `echo` "Zero".
Call your procedure with the number -5.
lessons.hint: Your `if` condition should be `num > 0`, `elif` should be `num < 0`, and `else` handles the rest.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: elif, C++: else if, Rust: else if. Nim's elif is identical to Python's. No parentheses needed around conditions.

docs.title: Tutorial - If Expressions
docs.summary: Conditional branching with if, elif, and else.

docs.title: Manual - If Statement
docs.summary: Conditional execution with if, elif, and else branches.

lessons.name: for Loops
lessons.concept:
Looping. `for i in a..b:` will loop from `a` to `b`, inclusive.
It's a simple, readable way to iterate.
lessons.task: Write a `for` loop that prints the numbers from 1 to 5, each on a new line.
lessons.solution:
for i in 1..5:
  echo i
lessons.hint: Use `for num in 1..5:`. Inside the loop, `echo num`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: for i in range(1, 6):. C++: for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++). Rust: for i in 1..=5 {}. Nim's 1..5 is inclusive like Rust's 1..=5.

docs.title: Tutorial - For Loops
docs.summary: Iteration with for loops over ranges, sequences, and strings.

docs.title: Manual - For Statement
docs.summary: Iteration over ranges, collections, and custom iterators.

lessons.name: while Loops
lessons.concept:
When you don't know how many iterations you need, use `while`.
`while condition:` keeps running as long as `condition` is true.
Be careful: if the condition never becomes false, you get an infinite loop.
lessons.task: Write a `while` loop that prints numbers from 1 to 3. Start with `var i = 1`. The loop should run while `i <= 3` and increment `i` each iteration.
lessons.solution:
var i = 1
while i <= 3:
  echo i
  i += 1
lessons.hint: `var i = 1`. Then `while i <= 3:`. Inside: `echo i`, then `i += 1`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Same as Python, C, Java. `while` loops are nearly identical across all C-family languages.

docs.title: Tutorial - While Loops
docs.summary: Conditional looping with while and related control flow.

docs.title: Manual - While Statement
docs.summary: Looping while a condition holds true.

lessons.name: break & continue
lessons.concept:
`break` exits a loop immediately. `continue` skips to the next iteration.
Use them sparingly — they can make control flow hard to follow. But sometimes they're exactly what you need.
Nim supports named loop blocks with `block label:` for breaking out of nested loops.
lessons.task: Write a `for` loop from 1 to 10. Use `continue` to skip even numbers (i mod 2 == 0). Use `break` to stop when you hit 7. Echo only the odd numbers before 7.
lessons.solution:
for i in 1..10:
  if i mod 2 == 0: continue
  if i == 7: break
  echo i
lessons.hint: Inside the loop: `if i mod 2 == 0: continue`, then `if i == 7: break`, then `echo i`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Same behavior as Python, C, Java. Nim additionally has `block label:` for breaking nested loops.

docs.title: Manual - Break Statement
docs.summary: Early exit from loops using break and named blocks.

docs.title: Manual - Continue Statement
docs.summary: Skipping to the next loop iteration with continue.

lessons.name: case/match
lessons.concept:
The `case` statement is Nim's version of a switch/match.
It works with integers, strings, chars, and enums.
Unlike C's `switch`, Nim's `case` doesn't fall through — you don't need `break` after each branch.
The `else:` branch handles unmatched values.
lessons.task: Write a `case` statement that takes an integer variable `day = 3`. If it's 1 or 2, echo "Weekday". If it's 6 or 7, echo "Weekend". Otherwise, echo "Midweek".
lessons.solution:
let day = 3
case day
of 1, 2:
  echo "Weekday"
of 6, 7:
  echo "Weekend"
else:
  echo "Midweek"
lessons.hint: `let day = 3`. Then `case day: of 1, 2: ... of 6, 7: ... else: ...`. No break needed.
lessons.cross_language_notes: C requires `break` in each case. Rust uses `match` with `=>` syntax and is exhaustive. Nim's `case` is like Rust's `match` in safety but with simpler syntax.

docs.title: Tutorial - Case Statement
docs.summary: Pattern matching with case/switch statements.

docs.title: Manual - Case Statement
docs.summary: Pattern matching with case and when statements.

lessons.name: Tuples
lessons.concept:
Tuples are fixed-size, ordered collections of values that can have different types.
They're useful for grouping related data without creating a full-blown object.
Syntax: `let myTuple = ("some string", 42)`.
You access their fields by index: `myTuple[0]`, `myTuple[1]`.
lessons.task: Declare a tuple named `person` containing a string "Alice" and an integer 30. Then, `echo` both fields, each on a new line.
lessons.hint: Declare it with `let person = ("Alice", 30)`. Then use `echo person[0]` and `echo person[1]`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python tuples use same syntax ("a", 1). C++ has std::pair and std::tuple. Rust uses ("a", 1). Nim's tuples are similar but support named fields too.

docs.title: Tutorial - Tuples
docs.summary: Fixed-size collections of mixed types, field access, and unpacking.

docs.title: Manual - Tuple Types
docs.summary: Fixed-size heterogeneous collections with named or positional fields.

lessons.name: Objects
lessons.concept:
Objects are custom data types that group named fields. They're like structs in other languages.
You define them with the `type` keyword.
type Person = object
  name: string
  age: int
lessons.task: Define a `Person` object type with a `name` (string) and `age` (int). Create an instance of it, but don't print anything.
lessons.solution:
type Person = object
  name: string
  age: int
let p = Person(name: "Bob", age: 25)
lessons.hint: After defining the type, create an instance: `let p = Person(name: "Bob", age: 25)`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: class Person: pass (reference semantics). C++: struct Person { ... } (value semantics). Rust: struct Person { ... } (value semantics). Nim's object is value type by default, like C++ and Rust.

docs.title: Tutorial Part 2 - Objects
docs.summary: Object-oriented programming in Nim: defining objects, fields, and methods.

docs.title: Manual - Object Types
docs.summary: Defining structured data types with fields and inheritance.

lessons.name: Sequences
lessons.concept:
Sequences, or `seq`, are dynamic-sized arrays. They're Nim's version of Python's lists or C++'s vectors.
You can add to them, remove from them, and they'll grow as needed.
Syntax: `var numbers = @[10, 20, 30]`.
lessons.task: Create a sequence of integers containing 1, 2, and 3. Use a `for` loop to iterate over it and `echo` each number.
lessons.solution:
var numbers = @[1, 2, 3]
for n in numbers: echo n
lessons.hint: Use `var numbers = @[1, 2, 3]` and then `for n in numbers: echo n`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: [1, 2, 3] (list). C++: std::vector<int>{1, 2, 3}. Rust: vec![1, 2, 3]. Nim uses @[1, 2, 3] — the @ sigil distinguishes seq from array.

docs.title: Tutorial - Sequences
docs.summary: Dynamic arrays (seq), creation, iteration, and common operations.

docs.title: Manual - Seq Type
docs.summary: Dynamic arrays in Nim: creation, indexing, and operations.

lessons.name: Enums & Case Statements
lessons.concept:
Enumerations (`enum`) are for when you have a variable that can only be one of a few possible values.
They're great for state machines and making your code type-safe.
The `case` statement is like a switch statement, perfect for handling each possible enum value.
lessons.task:
Define an enum `TrafficLight` with values `tlRed`, `tlYellow`, `tlGreen`.
Create a variable of this type and set it to `tlGreen`.
Use a `case` statement to `echo` "Go" if the light is green.
lessons.solution:
type TrafficLight = enum
  tlRed, tlYellow, tlGreen
let light = tlGreen
case light
of tlGreen:
  echo "Go"
else: discard
lessons.hint:
Define the enum, then: `let light = tlGreen`. The case statement looks like: `case light
of tlGreen:
  echo "Go"
else: discard`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: C enums are just integers (no type safety). Rust's enum is tagged (ADT). Nim's enum is closest to C in representation but provides type safety like Rust's simple enums.

docs.title: Tutorial - Enums
docs.summary: Enumeration types, ordinal values, and their usage in Nim.

docs.title: Manual - Enum Types
docs.summary: Enumeration types with ordinal values, custom values, and string conversion.

lessons.name: Option Type
lessons.concept:
What happens when a procedure might not be able to return a value? Use the `Option` type.
It's an enum that can be either `Some(value)` or `None`. This forces you to handle the 'no value' case at compile time, avoiding runtime errors.
You need to `import options` to use it.
lessons.task: Import `options`. Write a `proc` that returns `some(42)`. Use `get()` to unwrap the value and `echo` it.
lessons.hint: Start with `import options`. Your proc should be `proc getValue(): Option[int] = some(42)`. Then call it and use `.get()` to extract the value.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Rust: Option<T> with Some/None. C++17: std::optional. Python: None or raise. Nim's Option type is closest to Rust's — both require explicit unwrapping.

docs.title: std/options
docs.summary: Optional values with some/none pattern for nullable results.

lessons.name: The Gatekeeper
lessons.concept:
Time to put it all together. You're building a simple RPG party system.
You'll need Enums, Objects, Sequences, and Procs.
This is a comprehensive test of Phase 1.
lessons.task:
1. Define an Enum `Class` with values `Warrior`, `Mage`, `Rogue`.
2. Define an Object `Character` with `name` (string), `class` (Class), and `level` (int).
3. Create a sequence of Characters named `party`.
4. Add a Level 10 Warrior named "Gimli" and a Level 8 Mage named "Gandalf" to the party.
5. Iterate through the party and echo "[Name] the [Class] is level [Level]" for each.
lessons.validation_code: "Gimli the Warrior is level 10" in result.stdout and "Gandalf the Mage is level 8" in result.stdout
lessons.solution:
type
  Class = enum Warrior, Mage, Rogue
  Character = object
    name: string
    class: Class
    level: int
var party: seq[Character]
party.add(Character(name: "Gimli", class: Warrior, level: 10))
party.add(Character(name: "Gandalf", class: Mage, level: 8))
for c in party:
  echo c.name, " the ", c.class, " is level ", c.level
lessons.hint: Break it down. Enum first. Then Object. Then `var party: seq[Character]`. Then `party.add(...)`. Finally the loop.
lessons.cross_language_notes: This boss lesson combines concepts common to all OOP languages. Python: class + list. C++: struct + vector. Rust: struct + Vec. Nim's approach is closest to Rust — separate type definitions composed together.

lessons.name: String Basics
lessons.concept:
Strings in Nim are sequences of characters. They're mutable (you can modify individual chars) and dynamically sized.
Declare them with `var s = "hello"` or `var s: string = "hello"`.
Nim strings are NOT null-terminated (unlike C). They know their own length.
lessons.task: Declare a string variable `greeting` with the value "Nim" and echo it.
lessons.solution:
let greeting = "Nim"
echo greeting
lessons.hint: `let greeting = "Nim"` then `echo greeting`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python strings are immutable. C strings are char pointers. Nim strings are mutable and track their own length.

lessons.name: String Interpolation
lessons.concept:
Nim's `strformat` module gives you `fmt` for string interpolation.
Use `fmt"Value: {variable}"` to embed expressions directly.
This is cleaner than concatenating with `&`.
lessons.task: Import `strformat`. Declare `let name = "World"; let score = 100`. Use `fmt` to echo "Hello World, score: 100".
lessons.solution:
import strformat
let name = "World"
let score = 100
echo fmt"Hello {name}, score: {score}"
lessons.hint: `import strformat`. Then `let name = "World"`, `let score = 100`. Then `echo fmt"Hello {name}, score: {score}"`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python has f-strings (`f"..."`). C++ uses fmtlib. Rust uses `println!("...")`. Nim's `fmt` is closest to Python's f-strings.

docs.title: std/strformat
docs.summary: Compile-time string interpolation with the fmt macro.

lessons.name: String Operations
lessons.concept:
The `strutils` module provides rich string operations.
Common ones: `len()`, `contains()`, `split()`, `join()`, `strip()`, `toUpperAscii()`, `toLowerAscii()`.
Import it explicitly to use these.
lessons.task: Import `strutils`. Declare `let text = "A,B,C"`. Use `split(",")` to split it into a sequence, then use `join("-")` to join it back. Echo the result.
lessons.hint: Start with `import strutils`. Then `let text = "A,B,C"`. Then use `text.split(",").join("-")` and `echo` it.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: `.split()`, `.join()` — nearly identical. JavaScript: `.split()`, `.join()`. Nim follows the same pattern.

docs.title: std/strutils
docs.summary: String manipulation: split, join, strip, formatting, case conversion.

lessons.name: Multi-line & Raw Strings
lessons.concept:
Nim supports multi-line string literals with triple quotes `"""..."""`.
These can span multiple lines in your source code.
Raw strings use the `r` prefix: `r"C:\Users\name"` — backslashes are literal, not escape sequences.
lessons.task: Create a multi-line string using `"""` that contains three lines: "one", "two", "three" on separate lines. Echo it.
lessons.validation_code: "one" in result.stdout and "two" in result.stdout and "three" in result.stdout
lessons.solution:
let s = """one
two
three"""
echo s
lessons.hint: Use triple quotes `"""` to start and end. Put each word on its own line between them. Then `echo s`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python also uses triple quotes. C++ uses raw string literals `R"()"`. Rust uses `r#"..."#`.

docs.title: Manual - String Literals
docs.summary: String syntax: escape sequences, raw strings, triple-quoted multiline strings.

lessons.name: Creating Objects
lessons.concept:
Objects group related data into a single value.
Define them with the `type` keyword and `object` annotation.
A `Person` object with `name` and `age` is a common first example.
lessons.task: Define a `Book` object type with fields `title: string` and `pages: int`. Create an instance with title "Nim in Action" and pages 300. Echo the title.
lessons.solution:
type Book = object
  title: string
  pages: int
let b = Book(title: "Nim in Action", pages: 300)
echo b.title
lessons.hint: `type Book = object`. Then `let b = Book(title: ..., pages: ...)`. Access with `b.title`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python classes need `def __init__`. C++ has constructors. Rust uses structs with field init shorthand. Nim's object creation is closest to Rust.

docs.title: Tutorial Part 2 - Objects
docs.summary: Object-oriented programming in Nim: defining objects, fields, and methods.

docs.title: Manual - Object Types
docs.summary: Defining structured data types with fields and inheritance.

lessons.name: Object Fields & Methods
lessons.concept:
Objects can have any number of fields. Access them with dot notation: `obj.field`.
Nim doesn't have methods attached to objects — instead you write procs that take the object as the first argument.
Method call syntax (`obj.procName()`) makes it look like method calls.
lessons.task: Define a `Rectangle` object with `width: float` and `height: float`. Write a `proc area(r: Rectangle): float = r.width * r.height`. Create a rect with width 3.0, height 4.0 and call `area` using method syntax.
lessons.hint: Define the proc: `proc area(r: Rectangle): float = r.width * r.height`. Then call it: `r.area()` and `echo`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python uses `def area(self)` inside the class. C++ uses member functions. Nim separates data objects from procedures that act on them.

docs.title: Manual - Method Call Syntax
docs.summary: Calling procs with obj.method() syntax for cleaner code.

docs.title: Tutorial - Procedures
docs.summary: Defining and calling procedures, parameters, return types, and recursion.

lessons.name: Enums in Depth
lessons.concept:
Enums aren't just labels — they have an underlying `int` value.
You can access the `ord` (ordinal) and `$` (string representation).
You can also specify custom values: `enum MyEnum = val1, val2 = 10, val3`.
lessons.task: Define an enum `Color` with values `cRed`, `cGreen`, `cBlue`. Create a variable `paint` set to `cGreen`. Echo the string representation of `paint` (using `$`) and its ordinal (using `ord`), each on separate lines.
lessons.solution:
type Color = enum cRed, cGreen, cBlue
let paint = cGreen
echo $paint
echo ord(paint)
lessons.hint: `$paint` gives the string "cGreen". `ord(paint)` gives the integer 1 (0-indexed).
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python's `IntEnum` gives `.value` for the ordinal. C enums are just integers. Rust can `as isize` cast. Nim's `ord` and `$` are explicit and clean.

docs.title: Manual - Enum Types
docs.summary: Enumeration types with ordinal values, custom values, and string conversion.

docs.title: Tutorial - Enums
docs.summary: Enumeration types, ordinal values, and their usage in Nim.

lessons.name: Case with Enums
lessons.concept:
Enums and `case` work together naturally.
The compiler warns if you don't handle all possible enum values (exhaustiveness check).
This is a powerful pattern for state machines and command dispatch.
lessons.task: Define an enum `Direction` with values `dNorth`, `dSouth`, `dEast`, `dWest`. Create a `Direction` variable set to `dNorth`. Use a `case` statement: if North, echo "Up"; if South, echo "Down"; otherwise echo "Side".
lessons.solution:
type Direction = enum dNorth, dSouth, dEast, dWest
let dir = dNorth
case dir
of dNorth: echo "Up"
of dSouth: echo "Down"
else: echo "Side"
lessons.hint: Define the enum. `case dir: of dNorth: ... of dSouth: ... else: ...`. No `break` needed.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Rust's `match` is exhaustive by default. C's switch isn't. Nim's `case` with enums gives you compile-time exhaustiveness checks.

docs.summary: Pattern matching with case/switch statements.

docs.summary: Enumeration types with ordinal values, custom values, and string conversion.

lessons.name: Generics
lessons.concept:
Generics let you write procedures that can work with different types.
Instead of a specific type like `int`, you use a placeholder, like `T`.
Syntax: `proc echoFirst[T](items: seq[T]) =`
lessons.task: Write a generic procedure `echoFirst` that takes a sequence of any type and echoes the first element. Call it with a sequence of strings `@[ "a", "b"]`.
lessons.solution:
proc echoFirst[T](items: seq[T]) = echo items[0]
echoFirst(@["a", "b"])
lessons.hint: Define the proc as `proc echoFirst[T](items: seq[T]) = echo items[0]`. Then call it.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Java: generics with type erasure. C++: templates. Rust: generics with trait bounds. Nim's generics are closest to Rust's — type-checked at compile time with no runtime overhead.

docs.title: Tutorial Part 2 - Generics
docs.summary: Generic procedures and types for reusable, type-safe code.

docs.title: Manual - Generics
docs.summary: Generic procedures, types, and constraints.

lessons.name: Method Call Syntax
lessons.concept:
Nim lets you call procedures in two ways: `procName(obj, arg)` or `obj.procName(arg)`.
The second one is called 'method call syntax' and is just syntactic sugar. It makes code look more object-oriented.
lessons.task:
Define a `Person` object and a `proc greet(p: Person)` that echoes "Hello, " followed by the person's name.
Create a `Person` instance and call `greet` using method call syntax.
lessons.solution:
type Person = object
  name: string
proc greet(p: Person) = echo "Hello, ", p.name
let p = Person(name: "Carly")
p.greet()
lessons.hint: Define your proc, create a person `let p = Person(name: "Carly")`, then call the proc like this: `p.greet()`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: C++: obj.method() naturally. Rust: obj.method() via implicit self. Nim's method call syntax is pure sugar — the compiler rewrites obj.proc() to proc(obj).

docs.title: Manual - Method Call Syntax
docs.summary: Calling procs with obj.method() syntax for cleaner code.

lessons.name: Iterators
lessons.concept:
An `iterator` is a procedure that can 'yield' a value and be resumed later, making it perfect for custom loops.
They're memory-efficient because they produce values one at a time, instead of all at once.
lessons.task: Write an iterator `countTo(n: int)` that yields numbers from 1 up to `n`. Use it in a `for` loop to `echo` numbers up to 3.
lessons.solution:
iterator countTo(n: int): int =
  var i = 1
  while i <= n:
    yield i
    i += 1
for i in countTo(3): echo i
lessons.hint: Define it as `iterator countTo(n: int): int = ...`. Inside, use a `var` counter and a `while` loop that `yield`s the value.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: generator with yield. C#: iterator with yield return. Rust: impl Iterator. Nim's iterator syntax is closest to Python's generators.

docs.title: Manual - Iterators
docs.summary: Defining custom iterators with yield for lazy sequences.

lessons.name: Generic Constraints
lessons.concept:
Nim uses type classes like `SomeInteger` to constrain generic parameters.
These are sets of types that support certain operations.
`SomeInteger` includes all signed/unsigned ints but not `float`.
Use them to restrict a generic proc to only valid types.
lessons.task: Write a generic proc `sumInts[T: SomeInteger](seq: openArray[T]): T` that sums a sequence. Call it with `@[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` and echo the result.
lessons.solution:
proc sumInts[T: SomeInteger](seq: openArray[T]): T =
  var total: T = 0
  for x in seq: total += x
  total
echo sumInts(@[1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
lessons.hint: The proc signature is `proc sumInts[T: SomeInteger](seq: openArray[T]): T`. Inside: `var total: T = 0`, loop and add, return total.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Rust uses trait bounds like `T: Add<Output=T>`. C++20 uses `std::integral`. Nim uses type classes like `SomeInteger` which are structurally defined.

docs.title: Manual - Type Classes
docs.summary: Constraining generic parameters with type classes like SomeInteger.

lessons.name: Multiple Generic Params
lessons.concept:
Generic procs can take several independent type parameters, e.g., `proc pair[A, B](a: A, b: B)`. This is useful for heterogeneous containers or adapters.
The compiler infers the types at the call site.
lessons.task: Write a proc `makePair[A, B](x: A, y: B): string` that returns `"($1, $2)"` using string representation of both values. Call it with `42` and `"hello"`. Use `$` to convert.
lessons.solution:
proc makePair[A, B](x: A, y: B): string = "(" & $x & ", " & $y & ")"
echo makePair(42, "hello")
lessons.hint: The proc signature: `proc makePair[A, B](x: A, y: B): string`. Use `$` to convert each param to string.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python just accepts any types. C++ templates do this implicitly. Rust uses `fn pair<A, B>(a: A, b: B)`. Nim's syntax is clean and the compiler infers types.

docs.title: Manual - Generics
docs.summary: Generic procedures, types, and constraints.

lessons.name: Generic Objects & Procs
lessons.concept:
In Nim, generics allow you to write flexible and reusable code. A generic procedure can operate on different types, as long as those types support the operations the procedure performs. For instance, you can write a generic procedure to sum elements of a list, provided the elements are numeric. Here's a quick example of a generic `sum` proc:

```nim
proc sum[T](list: seq[T]): T =
  var total: T = 0
  for item in list:
    total += item
  return total
```

This `sum` proc can take a list of integers, floats, or any other numeric type and return the sum of its elements. The key here is the type variable `T`, which is constrained to be a numeric type. This ensures that the operations inside the proc are valid for the given type.
lessons.task:
Your task is to write a generic procedure named `sum` that takes a list of any numeric type and returns the sum of its elements. The procedure should work for both integers and floats. Here's what you need to do:

1. Define a generic procedure `sum` with a type variable `T`.
2. Ensure that `T` is constrained to be a numeric type.
3. Initialize a variable `total` of type `T` to 0.
4. Iterate over each element in the list, adding each element to `total`.
5. Return the `total` after the loop.

Your solution should pass the following test cases:

- `sum([1, 2, 3, 4])` should return `10`.
- `sum([1.5, 2.5, 3.5])` should return `7.5`.
lessons.hint: Remember, the key is to define a type variable `T` and ensure it's constrained to be a numeric type. You can use the `static` keyword to enforce this constraint. Also, make sure to initialize `total` correctly and iterate over the list to accumulate the sum.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Use a generic function with type hints. Rust equivalent: Use a generic function with trait bounds. C++ equivalent: Use templates with type constraints.

docs.title: Manual - Generic Objects
docs.summary: Objects with generic type parameters for reusable data structures.

docs.title: Tutorial Part 2 - Generics
docs.summary: Generic procedures and types for reusable, type-safe code.

lessons.name: Paths & Directory Walking
lessons.concept:
Working with the filesystem is table stakes for any serious programming language. Nim's `std/os` module gives you everything you need without ceremony.

Key procs:
- `walkDir(dir)` — iterates entries in a directory (non-recursive)
- `walkDirRec(dir)` — recursively walks a directory tree
- `existsDir(path)` / `existsFile(path)` — check existence and type
- `getCurrentDir()` — returns the current working directory
- `joinPath(a, b)` — platform-correct path joining
- `splitPath(path)` — splits into (head, tail)
- `parentDir(path)` — returns the parent directory
- `relativePath(path, base)` — get relative path from base

Rule of thumb: Always use `joinPath` or `/` operator (from `std/os`) instead of string concatenation. Windows uses backslashes; your string concat will break.
lessons.task: Write a proc `countNimFiles(dir: string): int` that counts all `.nim` files recursively in a directory. Call it with `"."` and echo the count.
lessons.hint: Use `walkDirRec(dir)` for recursive iteration, then check `path.endsWith(".nim")`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: `pathlib.Path(dir).rglob('*.nim')`. Rust: `WalkDir::new(dir)`. Nim's `walkDirRec` is the simplest of the three — one call, no chaining.

docs.title: std/os
docs.summary: Operating system interface: file operations, env vars, process info, paths.

lessons.name: File Operations
lessons.concept:
Nim's `std/os` includes all the file operations you'd expect: copy, move, delete, symlink, and metadata queries.

Key procs:
- `copyFile(src, dest)` — copies a file
- `moveFile(src, dest)` — moves/renames a file
- `createSymlink(src, dest)` — creates a symbolic link
- `removeFile(path)` — deletes a file
- `removeDir(path)` — deletes an empty directory
- `removeDir(path, checkDir=false)` — deletes directory tree
- `getFileSize(path)` — returns file size in bytes
- `getLastModificationTime(path)` — returns last modified `Time`
- `fileExists(path)` / `dirExists(path)` — existence checks
- `tempfile()` — creates a temporary file and returns its path

Always check existence before operating. `copyFile` will fail if the source doesn't exist.
lessons.task: Write a proc `backupFile(path: string): string` that copies a file to `path.bak` if it exists, returning the backup path or empty string. Call it with `"nimlings.nimble"` and echo the result.
lessons.solution:
import std/os
proc backupFile(path: string): string =
  if fileExists(path):
    let bak = path & ".bak"
    copyFile(path, bak)
    return bak
  return ""
echo backupFile("nimlings.nimble")
lessons.hint: Check `fileExists`, then `copyFile` to `path & ".bak"`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: `shutil.copy2(path, path + '.bak')`. Rust: `fs::copy(path, format!('{}.bak', path))`. Nim's `copyFile` is stdlib — no extra import needed.

docs.title: std/os
docs.summary: Operating system interface: file operations, env vars, process info, paths.

lessons.name: Process Management
lessons.concept:
Sometimes you need to run external commands. Nim's `std/osproc` gives you clean wrappers around process creation.

Key procs:
- `execCmd(cmd)` — runs a command, returns exit code
- `execCmdEx(cmd)` — runs a command, returns `(output, exitCode)` tuple
- `startProcess(cmd, args)` — full control over stdin/stdout/stderr with a Process handle

`execCmdEx` is your go-to for most tasks. It captures stdout and stderr (merged), gives you the exit code, and blocks until the process finishes.

For long-running processes, use `startProcess` with `inputStream`, `outputStream`, and `waitForExit(timeout)` to avoid hanging indefinitely.
lessons.task: Write a proc `runAndCapture(cmd: string): tuple[output: string, exitCode: int]` that runs a shell command and returns output + exit code. Call it with `"nim --version"` and echo the output.
lessons.solution:
import std/osproc
proc runAndCapture(cmd: string): tuple[output: string, exitCode: int] = execCmdEx(cmd)
let (outp, code) = runAndCapture("nim --version")
echo outp
lessons.hint: Use `execCmdEx(cmd)` which returns `(output, exitCode)` tuple — one call, done.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: `subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, shell=True)`. Rust: `Command::new("sh").arg(cmd).output()`. Nim's `execCmdEx` is the simplest stdlib wrapper.

docs.title: std/osproc
docs.summary: External process execution: spawn, communicate, capture output.

lessons.name: Temp Files & Directories
lessons.concept:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to create temporary directories and write files to them using Nim. This is useful for creating temporary storage for data that you don't want to keep around after your program finishes. Remember, the `os` module has all the tools you need to handle directories and files. Here's a quick example of creating a temp directory and writing a file to it:

```nim
import os

let tempDir = createTempDir()
echo tempDir

let filePath = joinPath(tempDir, "tempfile.txt")
writeFile(filePath, "Hello, temp file!")
echo filePath
```

This code creates a temporary directory, writes a file to it, and prints out the paths for verification.
lessons.task: Your task is to create a temporary directory and write a file named `data.txt` to it. The content of the file should be `Nim is awesome!`. After writing the file, print the path of the temporary directory and the file path to verify that everything is in place.
lessons.hint: Remember to use `createTempDir` to make a temp directory and `writeFile` to write to a file. Don't forget to print the paths to verify your work.
lessons.validation_code: result.exitCode == 0 and "Temporary Directory: " in result.stdout and "File Path: " in result.stdout
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: Use `tempfile` module; Rust: Use `tempfile` crate; C++: Use `std::filesystem` for directory creation and file writing.

docs.title: std/os
docs.summary: Operating system interface: file operations, env vars, process info, paths.

lessons.name: Date & Time Basics
lessons.concept:
Nim's `std/times` module handles dates, times, and durations. It's comprehensive but the API naming has some quirks.

Key types:
- `DateTime` — a date + time with timezone info
- `Time` — a Unix timestamp (seconds since epoch)
- `Duration` — a span of time (nanosecond precision)
- `Month` — enum: `mJan` through `mDec`
- `WeekDay` — enum: `dMon` through `dSun`

Key procs:
- `now()` — returns current `DateTime`
- `dt.format(fmt)` — formats as string. Format tokens use lowercase: `yyyy`, `MM`, `dd`, `HH`, `mm`, `ss`
- `parse(dateStr, fmt)` — parses a string into `DateTime`
- `toUnix(dt)` / `fromUnix(t)` — convert between `DateTime` and `Time`

⚠️ Nim uses lowercase `yyyy` (not `YYYY` like Python). Get it wrong and your output will look like garbage.
lessons.task: Write a proc `formatDate(dt: DateTime): string` that formats a DateTime as `"YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm"`. Call it with `now()` and echo the result.
lessons.hint: Use `dt.format("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm")` — Nim uses lowercase yyyy unlike Python's %Y.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: `dt.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')`. Rust: `dt.format('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')`. Nim uses lowercase yyyy (not YYYY).

docs.title: std/times
docs.summary: Date/time handling: DateTime, Duration, formatting, and arithmetic.

lessons.name: Date Arithmetic
lessons.concept:
Nim's `std/times` module provides date arithmetic through simple `+` and `-` operators. Subtracting two `DateTime` objects returns a `Duration`.
Use `.inDays`, `.inSeconds`, or `.inMinutes` to extract the desired unit.
Create dates with `initDateTime(day, month, year, hour, minute, second, zone)`.
lessons.task: Write a proc `daysUntilNewYear(): int` that returns the number of days from now until January 1 of next year. Echo the result.
lessons.hint: Create a future date with `initDateTime(1, mJan, year+1, 0, 0, 0, utc())`, subtract `now()`, and get `.inDays`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: `(datetime(year+1, 1, 1) - datetime.now()).days`. Rust: `Utc.ymd(year+1, 1, 1).signed_duration_since(Utc::now()).num_days()`. Nim uses `.inDays` on Duration.

docs.title: std/times
docs.summary: Date/time handling: DateTime, Duration, formatting, and arithmetic.

lessons.name: Timezones
lessons.concept:
Timezones are a way to represent the local time in different regions of the world. They account for the difference in hours from Coordinated Universal Time (UTC). In Nim, you can handle timezones using the `times` module, which provides functions to manipulate and convert times. Here's a quick example of how you might convert a UTC time to a local time with a specified offset:

```nim
import times

let utcTime = parseInstant("2023-10-01T12:00:00Z")
let offset = 5  # UTC+5
let localTime = utcTime + offset.hours

echo localTime
```

This code snippet parses a UTC time, adds a 5-hour offset, and prints the local time. Notice how we use the `hours` method to convert the offset into a `Time` object that can be added to the `Instant`.

Remember, the key is to correctly parse the time, apply the offset, and ensure the output is in the expected format.
lessons.task:
Write a Nim program that converts a given UTC time to a local time using a specified timezone offset. Your program should take two inputs: a UTC time string and an integer representing the timezone offset in hours. It should then output the local time.

**Expected Output:**
```
2023-10-01T17:00:00
```

**Note:** The input time should be in the format `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ` (ISO 8601) and the offset should be an integer representing the number of hours to add to the UTC time.
lessons.solution:
import times

proc convertUtcToLocalTime(utcTimeStr: string, offset: int): string =
  let utcTime = parseInstant(utcTimeStr)
  let localTime = utcTime + offset.hours
  return $localTime

let utcTimeStr = "2023-10-01T12:00:00Z"
let offset = 5
let localTime = convertUtcToLocalTime(utcTimeStr, offset)

echo localTime
lessons.hint: Don't forget to parse the UTC time string correctly and use the `hours` method to apply the offset. The `times` module has everything you need to handle this task.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: datetime module / Rust equivalent: chrono crate / C++ equivalent: time library

docs.title: std/times
docs.summary: Date/time handling: DateTime, Duration, formatting, and arithmetic.

lessons.name: Defining Operators
lessons.concept: In Nim, you can define custom operators to extend the language's syntax. For instance, you can create an operator to concatenate two strings with a space in between. This is useful for making your code more readable and expressive. Here's a quick example: `let result = "hello" & "world"`. This will output `hello world`. Note that the `&` operator is already defined for strings in Nim, but for the sake of this exercise, we'll define our own.
lessons.task: Define a custom operator `~` that concatenates two strings with a space in between. Your operator should work like this: `let result = "hello" ~ "world"`. The expected output should be `hello world`.
lessons.solution:
import strutils

proc `~`(a, b: string): string =
  return a & " " & b

let result = "hello" ~ "world"
echo result
lessons.hint: Remember, you need to define a proc with the name of your operator. The operator should take two strings as arguments and return a new string with a space in between. Don't forget to use the `&` operator to concatenate strings.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, you can achieve similar functionality using the `+` operator with a space: `result = str1 + ' ' + str2`. In Rust, you can use the `format!` macro: `let result = format!("{} {}", str1, str2);`. In C++, you can use the `+` operator with `std::string` and `std::string::operator+=`: `std::string result = str1 + " " + str2;`.

docs.title: Manual - Operators
docs.summary: Custom operator definitions and precedence rules.

lessons.name: Operator Precedence
lessons.concept:
In Nim, operator precedence determines the order in which operations are performed in an expression. Operators with higher precedence are evaluated before those with lower precedence. For instance, in the expression `2 * 3 + 4`, the multiplication is performed before the addition because the multiplication operator (`*`) has higher precedence than the addition operator (`+`). If you want to create a custom operator that calculates the power of a number and has higher precedence than the multiplication operator (`*`), you can define it using the `^` operator, which is already used for exponentiation in Nim. However, if you want to create a custom operator with a different symbol, you need to ensure it has the correct precedence. This can be achieved by defining the operator with a first character that corresponds to a higher precedence level in the precedence table. For example, operators starting with `$` or `^` have the highest precedence level (10). Here's a quick example of how you might define a custom power operator with higher precedence than multiplication:

```nim
proc `**`(a, b: int): int =
  result = a ^ b
```

This defines a custom operator `**` that calculates the power of two integers. Note that the actual implementation uses the existing `^` operator for exponentiation, but the custom operator `**` will have higher precedence than `*`.
lessons.task: Create a custom operator in Nim that calculates the power of a number and has higher precedence than the multiplication operator. The operator should be named `**`. Write a Nim program that defines this operator and then evaluates the expressions `2 * 3 ** 2` and `2 ** 3 * 2`. The output should be `18` for the first expression and `54` for the second expression.
lessons.solution:
proc `**`(a, b: int): int =
  result = a ^ b

echo 2 * 3 ** 2  # Should output 18
echo 2 ** 3 * 2  # Should output 54
lessons.hint: Remember, the key to higher precedence is the first character of your operator. Since `^` already has the highest precedence, you can use it directly or define a new operator with a similar first character. Don't overthink it; just make sure your operator starts with `$` or `^` to ensure it's evaluated before `*`.
lessons.cross_language_notes:
Python equivalent: `**` operator
Rust equivalent: `**` operator
C++ equivalent: `pow` function or `**` operator (C++11 and later)

lessons.name: Custom Assignment
lessons.concept:
In Nim, you can define custom copy behavior using the `=copy` hook. This hook is called when an object is assigned to another, replacing the default copy semantics. For a 2D point object, you might want to add an offset when copying:

```nim
type
  Point = object
    x, y: float

proc `=copy`(dest: var Point; source: Point) =
  dest.x = source.x + 0.1
  dest.y = source.y + 0.1
```

This custom `=copy` adds 0.1 to both coordinates. Note: Overloading the `=` operator directly is deprecated in Nim 2.x — use `=copy` instead.
lessons.task:
Your task is to create a custom copy hook for a simple 2D point object. The `Point` object should have `x` and `y` coordinates. Your `=copy` hook should update the coordinates of the destination by adding a small offset (0.1) to both `x` and `y` when another point is assigned to it.

Write the complete Nim code for the `Point` object and the `=copy` hook. When you run the code, it should print the updated coordinates of the point after the copy.
lessons.solution:
type
  Point = object
    x, y: float

proc `=copy`(dest: var Point; source: Point) =
  dest.x = source.x + 0.1
  dest.y = source.y + 0.1

var p1 = Point(x: 1.0, y: 2.0)
var p2 = Point(x: 3.0, y: 4.0)

p1 = p2  # triggers =copy

echo "Updated p1: (", p1.x, ", ", p1.y, ")"
lessons.hint: The =copy hook is `proc =copy(dest: var Point; source: Point)`. Use object constructor syntax `Point(x: 1.0, y: 2.0)` not tuple syntax. Add 0.1 to both coordinates inside =copy.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, custom assignment operators are not directly supported. In Rust, you can implement the `Clone` trait for copy semantics. In C++, you can overload the assignment operator using `operator=` syntax. Nim uses `=copy` hooks which are similar to Rust's `Clone`.

lessons.name: Object Variants
lessons.concept:
Object variants in Nim allow you to define a type that can hold different kinds of data, similar to unions in other languages. This is useful for creating flexible data structures without the overhead of an object hierarchy. For example, you can define a `Calculator` object that can represent different operations like addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. Each operation can have its own set of fields, and the `kind` field acts as a discriminator to determine which fields are active. Accessing fields that don't match the current `kind` will raise a `FieldDefect` exception. Here's a simple example:

```nim
type
  OperationKind = enum
    opAdd, opSub, opMul, opDiv
  Calculator = ref object
    case kind: OperationKind
    of opAdd: (a, b: float)
    of opSub: (a, b: float)
    of opMul: (a, b: float)
    of opDiv: (a, b: float)
```

In this example, the `Calculator` object can represent either an addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division operation. The `kind` field determines which operation is active, and the corresponding fields (`a`, `b`) are used to store the operands.
lessons.task: Create a Nim program that defines an object variant for a simple calculator with operations like addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. The program should include a function to perform the calculation based on the operation kind. Additionally, the program should handle invalid field accesses by raising an exception. The program should output the result of a calculation when executed.
lessons.solution:
import strformat

type
  OperationKind = enum
    opAdd, opSub, opMul, opDiv
  Calculator = ref object
    case kind: OperationKind
    of opAdd: (a, b: float)
    of opSub: (a, b: float)
    of opMul: (a, b: float)
    of opDiv: (a, b: float)

proc calculate(calc: Calculator): float =
  case calc.kind
  of opAdd: return calc.a + calc.b
  of opSub: return calc.a - calc.b
  of opMul: return calc.a * calc.b
  of opDiv: return calc.a / calc.b

var calc = Calculator(kind: opAdd, a: 5.0, b: 3.0)

echo fmt"Result of calculation: {calculate(calc):.2f}"

This program defines a `Calculator` object variant with four possible operations. The `calculate` function performs the appropriate operation based on the `kind` field. The program then creates a `Calculator` object for addition and outputs the result.
lessons.hint: Remember, accessing fields that don't match the current `kind` will raise a `FieldDefect` exception. Make sure your `calculate` function handles all possible `kind` values.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Using classes with type hints and conditional checks. Rust equivalent: Using enums with associated data. C++ equivalent: Using unions with a discriminator field.

lessons.name: Multi-Methods
lessons.concept:
Multi-Methods in Nim allow you to define methods that can be dispatched based on the types of their arguments. This is similar to method overloading in other languages, but with more flexibility. For example, you can define a method that handles different types of expressions, like literals and addition expressions. Here's a quick example:

```nim
method eval(e: Expression): int {.base.} =
  quit "to override!"

method eval(e: Literal): int = e.x
method eval(e: PlusExpr): int = eval(e.a) + eval(e.b)
```

In this snippet, `eval` is a method that can handle different types of expressions. The `Literal` type represents a numeric value, and the `PlusExpr` type represents an addition expression. The `eval` method is overridden for each type to provide specific behavior.

To use multi-methods, you need to compile with the `--multimethods:on` flag.
lessons.task: Implement a simple calculator using multi-methods to handle different types of expressions. Your calculator should be able to evaluate expressions like `1 + 2` and `3 * (4 + 5)`. Define the necessary types and methods to handle literals, addition, and multiplication. The expected output for `1 + 2` should be `3`, and for `3 * (4 + 5)` should be `27`.
lessons.solution:
import strutils

# Define the base expression type
type
  Expression = ref object of RootObj
  Literal = ref object of Expression
    x: int
  PlusExpr = ref object of Expression
    a, b: Expression
  MultExpr = ref object of Expression
    a, b: Expression

# Base method for evaluation
method eval(e: Expression): int {.base.} =
  quit "to override!"

# Method to evaluate literals
method eval(e: Literal): int = e.x

# Method to evaluate addition expressions
method eval(e: PlusExpr): int = eval(e.a) + eval(e.b)

# Method to evaluate multiplication expressions
method eval(e: MultExpr): int = eval(e.a) * eval(e.b)

# Helper functions to create expressions
proc newLit(x: int): Literal = Literal(x: x)
proc newPlus(a, b: Expression): PlusExpr = PlusExpr(a: a, b: b)
proc newMult(a, b: Expression): MultExpr = MultExpr(a: a, b: b)

# Test the calculator
let expr1 = newPlus(newLit(1), newLit(2))
let expr2 = newMult(newLit(3), newPlus(newLit(4), newLit(5)))

echo eval(expr1)  # Expected output: 3
echo eval(expr2)  # Expected output: 27
lessons.hint: Remember, multi-methods are all about dynamic dispatch based on argument types. Define your expression types and override the `eval` method for each. Don't forget to compile with `--multimethods:on`!
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Use class methods with type hints. Rust equivalent: Use trait-based polymorphism. C++ equivalent: Use virtual functions in a class hierarchy.

docs.summary: Dynamic dispatch based on runtime types of multiple arguments.

lessons.name: Concepts (type classes)
lessons.concept:
In Nim, concepts are a powerful way to define a set of requirements that a type must satisfy. They can be used to create type classes, which are similar to interfaces in other languages. One common use case is to define a concept that checks if a type can be converted to a string. This is useful for implementing generic functions that need to log or display values.

For example, let's define a concept called `Stringable` that checks if a type can be converted to a string using the `$` operator. We can then use this concept to implement a logging function that works with any type that satisfies the `Stringable` concept.

```nim
# Define the Stringable concept
type
  Stringable = concept x
    $x is string
    return $x

# Implement the log function using the Stringable concept
proc log[T: Stringable](value: T) =
  echo value
```

In this example, the `log` function is generic and can take any type that satisfies the `Stringable` concept. When called, it simply echoes the string representation of the value.

This approach is similar to duck typing in Python, where a function checks if an object has a certain method (e.g., `__str__`) before using it. In Rust, this is akin to using trait bounds, and in C++, it might be implemented using template specialization with type traits.
lessons.task:
Your task is to create a concept called `Stringable` that checks if a type can be converted to a string using the `$` operator. Then, implement a logging function called `log` that takes any type that satisfies the `Stringable` concept and prints it to the console. Your function should handle different types like integers, strings, and custom objects that can be converted to strings.

Expected output:
```
42
Hello, world!
Custom object: value=100
```

Make sure your solution is complete and compilable.
lessons.solution:
# Define the Stringable concept
import strformat

type
  Stringable = concept x
    $x is string
    return $x

  CustomObject = object
    value: int

# Implement the log function using the Stringable concept
proc log[T: Stringable](value: T) =
  echo value

# Test the log function with different types
log(42)
log("Hello, world!")
log(CustomObject(value: 100))

This solution defines the `Stringable` concept to check if a type can be converted to a string. It then implements the `log` function to print any type that satisfies this concept. The function is tested with an integer, a string, and a custom object.
lessons.hint: Remember, the key is to define the `Stringable` concept correctly and use it in the `log` function. Don't forget to handle custom objects by implementing the `$` operator for them.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: duck typing with `__str__` method / Rust equivalent: trait bounds / C++ equivalent: template specialization with type traits

lessons.name: Void & auto Types
lessons.concept:
Nim's type system includes `auto` and `void` as special types.

`auto` on a proc return type tells the compiler to infer the return type from the body:
```nim
proc returnsInt(): auto = 1984
```

`void` as a return type is optional — a proc without a return type already returns `void`. Explicit `void` is redundant.

Note: Using `auto` on *parameters* is NOT standard Nim. It creates an implicit generic, which works in simple cases but is not idiomatic. Use generic procs with named type params instead:
```nim
proc printData[T](data: T) = echo data  # idiomatic
```

This lesson demonstrates `auto` return inference using a proc that prints any type via implicit generics.
lessons.task: Write a Nim proc that takes a single parameter and prints it. The proc should use `auto` return type inference (implicitly generic). Call it with an int, a string, and a bool. Expected output: `42\nhello\ntrue`.
lessons.solution:
proc printData(data: auto) = echo data

printData(42)
printData("hello")
printData(true)
lessons.hint: `auto` on the return type lets the compiler infer. Auto on a parameter makes it implicitly generic — works but prefer `proc foo[T](x: T)` for real code.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: Everything is dynamically typed, no auto needed. Rust: `impl Trait` for return type inference, generics for parameter polymorphism. C++: `auto` for return type deduction (C++14), templates for generic params. Nim's `auto` on return type is closest to C++14 auto; on parameters it's a quirk, not a language feature.

lessons.name: Ref Types
lessons.concept:
Sometimes you need multiple variables to point to the *same* object in memory.
A `ref` type is a reference to an object. When you pass a `ref`, you're not passing a copy, you're passing a pointer to the original.
This is how you get shared, mutable state.
lessons.task:
Define a `Person` object. Create a `ref Person` variable `p1`.
Create a second variable `p2` and assign `p1` to it.
Modify the `name` of the person through `p2`.
Echo the name through `p1` to see that it changed.
lessons.solution:
type Person = ref object
  name: string
var p1 = Person(name: "John")
var p2 = p1
p2.name = "Jane"
echo p1.name
lessons.hint: Use `type Person = ref object; var p1 = Person(name: "John"); var p2 = p1; p2.name = "Jane"; echo p1.name`
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: all objects are refs. C++: need std::shared_ptr. Rust: Rc<T> or Arc<T>. Nim's ref object is closest to Python's reference semantics.

lessons.name: Linked Lists with ref
lessons.concept:
`ref` objects let you build recursive data structures like linked lists.
A `Node` can have a `next: ref Node` field, creating a chain.
When you remove a node from the chain, the remaining nodes stay alive because they're still referenced.
lessons.task: Define a `Node` object with `data: int` and `next: ref Node`. Build a two-node chain (1 -> 2). Drop the first node by assigning `head = head.next`. Echo the data of the remaining node.
lessons.solution:
type Node = ref object
  data: int
  next: Node
var head = Node(data: 1)
head.next = Node(data: 2)
head = head.next
echo head.data
lessons.hint: `type Node = ref object`. Build the chain, then `head = head.next` to drop first node, then `echo head.data`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Similar to Python's normal objects (ref semantics). Unlike C++ where you'd use pointers or `std::shared_ptr`. Rust would require `Rc` or `Box`.

lessons.name: Raw Pointers with ptr
lessons.concept:
`ptr` is a raw, unchecked pointer. Use `addr` to get the address of a mutable variable.
Raw pointers bypass Nim's memory safety — they're mainly for FFI.
Dereference a `ptr` with `[]`: `p[]` gives you the value at the address.
lessons.task: Write a proc `swap(a, b: ptr int)` that swaps two integers using raw pointers. Use `let tmp = a[]; a[] = b[]; b[] = tmp`. Call it with `addr(x)` and `addr(y)` then echo x and y.
lessons.validation_code: "x=10 y=20" in result.stdout or "x=10" in result.stdout
lessons.solution:
proc swap(a, b: ptr int) =
  let tmp = a[]
  a[] = b[]
  b[] = tmp
var x = 20
var y = 10
swap(addr(x), addr(y))
echo "x=", x, " y=", y
lessons.hint: Use `addr(x)` to get the pointer. Dereference with `[]`. The swap: save tmp, assign b to a, assign tmp to b.
lessons.cross_language_notes: C's `int*`, C++'s raw pointers. Rust requires `unsafe`. Nim's `ptr` is typed like C pointers.

lessons.name: Move Semantics
lessons.concept:
With `--gc:arc` or `--gc:orc`, `owned` indicates unique ownership. A `owned T` can be moved but not copied.
Use `move(x)` from `std/sugar` to transfer ownership.
After moving, the original variable is consumed and can't be used.
lessons.task: Import `std/sugar`. Create a `ref object` `Data` with a `value: string`. Write a proc `take(owned: owned Data)` that prints `owned.value`. Create a `Data` instance, move it into `take`, and echo.
lessons.solution:
import std/sugar
type Data = ref object
  value: string
proc take(owned: owned Data) = echo owned.value
var d = Data(value: "Hello")
take(move(d))
lessons.hint: `type Data = ref object`. The proc takes `owned: owned Data`. Call with `take(move(d))`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Similar to C++ `std::move` or Rust's move by default. Nim requires explicit `owned` and `move()`.

lessons.name: Destructors (=destroy)
lessons.concept:
In Nim, destructors are special procedures that are automatically called when an object goes out of scope. They're defined using the `=destroy` keyword. This is useful for cleaning up resources or performing final actions before an object is destroyed. For example, if you have an object that opens a file, you can use a destructor to ensure the file is closed when the object is no longer needed. Here's a simple example:

```nim
type
  MyObject = object
    name: string

proc `=destroy`(o: var MyObject) =
  echo "Object destroyed"

proc `=create`(o: var MyObject) =
  o.name = "MyObject"
  echo "Object created"

var obj = MyObject()
```

In this example, when `obj` goes out of scope, the `=destroy` procedure is called, printing 'Object destroyed'.
lessons.task: Write a Nim program that defines a custom object with a destructor. The object should have a field `name` of type `string`. When the object is created, print 'Object created'. When the object is destroyed, print 'Object destroyed'. Demonstrate the usage of the destructor by creating an instance of the object and letting it go out of scope.
lessons.solution:
type
  MyObject = object
    name: string

proc `=destroy`(o: var MyObject) =
  echo "Object destroyed"

proc `=create`(o: var MyObject) =
  o.name = "MyObject"
  echo "Object created"

var obj = MyObject()
lessons.hint: Remember, the destructor is automatically called when the object goes out of scope. Just create an instance of your object and let it go out of scope to see the destructor in action.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, destructors are not explicitly defined; instead, the `__del__` method is used. In Rust, destructors are implemented using the `Drop` trait. In C++, destructors are defined using the `~` operator.

docs.title: Manual - Destructors

lessons.name: Copy Constructors (=copy)
lessons.concept:
In Nim, the `=copy` hook is used to implement custom copy constructors for types. This ensures that resources are properly duplicated when an object is assigned to another. The general pattern involves checking for self-assignments, destroying the destination object, marking it as moved, and then duplicating the source's resources. Here's an example:

```nim
proc `=copy`(dest: var T; source: T) =
  if dest.field != source.field:
    `=destroy`(dest)
    wasMoved(dest)
    dest.field = duplicateResource(source.field)
```

This pattern ensures that the destination object is properly cleaned up before being duplicated, preventing resource leaks and ensuring that self-assignments are handled correctly.
lessons.task: Implement a custom copy constructor for a simple Nim type. The type should have a single field that is a string. Ensure that the copy constructor properly duplicates the string resource and handles self-assignments. The main procedure should create two instances of the type, assign one to the other, and print the contents of both instances to verify that the copy was successful.
lessons.solution:
type
  MyType = object
    field: string

proc `=copy`(dest: var MyType; source: MyType) =
  if dest.field != source.field:
    `=destroy`(dest)
    wasMoved(dest)
    dest.field = source.field

proc main() =
  var a = MyType(field: "Hello")
  var b = a
  echo "a.field: ", a.field
  echo "b.field: ", b.field

main()
lessons.hint: The `=copy` hook receives `dest: var MyType` and `source: MyType`. Handle self-assignment by comparing fields, destroy the destination, mark it moved with `wasMoved`, then copy the field. Strings are value types so direct assignment copies them.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, deep copying is handled by the `copy` module. In Rust, copy semantics are enforced by the `Copy` trait. In C++, copy constructors are explicitly defined to handle deep copying.

docs.title: Manual - Copy Constructors
docs.summary: Custom copy behavior with =copy hooks for resource management.

lessons.name: Sink Parameters (sink)
lessons.concept:
A `sink` parameter marks a parameter as moved-into; it tells Nim the caller won't need the value afterward.

This enables the compiler to elide copies (and associated destructor work). When a sink parameter is passed a mutable location, the compiler rewrites the call to move rather than copy - similar to C++'s std::move but automatic.

Key rules:
- Sink parameters are declared with `sink` before the type name.
- Inside the proc, the sink parameter behaves as a mutable local.
- The caller's variable becomes invalid after the call.

Sink parameters compose naturally with destructors and copy constructors to build efficient, safe resource management.
lessons.task: Explain the purpose of `sink` parameters and when to use them. Show a code example demonstrating sink parameter syntax and its effect on ownership transfer.
lessons.hint: Think of sink as a 'you break it, you own it' tag. Declare with `proc foo(x: sink T) = ...`. After the call, the original variable is consumed -- you can't use it anymore without a compiler error.
lessons.cross_language_notes: C++: Similar to std::move semantics but automatic for sink parameters. Rust: Sink is less strict than Rust's ownership -- it's a borrowing convention optimized by the compiler rather than enforced by the type system.

lessons.name: Try/Except
lessons.concept:
When things go wrong, Nim raises exceptions. You can handle them with a `try`/`except` block.
This prevents your program from crashing when something unexpected happens, like accessing a bad array index.
lessons.task: Create a small array. In a `try` block, attempt to access an index that is out of bounds. In the `except IndexDefect` block, `echo` the message "Bad index!".
lessons.hint:
The structure is `try:
  discard a[10]
except IndexDefect:
  echo "Bad index!"`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: try/except. C++: try/catch. Rust: Result<T, E> (no exceptions). Nim uses exceptions like Python and C++, but also supports Result types via options.

lessons.name: Custom Exceptions
lessons.concept:
Exceptions inherit from `CatchableError` (recoverable) or `Defect` (unrecoverable).
Define your own with `type MyError = object of CatchableError`.
You can add extra fields to carry error info.
lessons.task: Define `DivideByZeroError` as an object of `CatchableError`. Write a proc `safeDiv(a, b: int): int` that raises `DivideByZeroError` when `b == 0`. Its `msg` field should be "cannot divide by zero".
lessons.hint: `type DivideByZeroError = ref object of CatchableError`. In `safeDiv`, `if b == 0: raise ...`. In main: `try: ... except: echo "caught"`.

lessons.name: The defer Statement
lessons.concept:
`defer` schedules code to run when the current scope exits — useful for guaranteed cleanup.
It's more concise than `try/finally` and runs even if an exception is raised.
Place `defer` right after acquiring a resource.
lessons.task: Write a proc that opens a file for writing, uses `defer: f.close()` right after opening, writes "hello", and then echoes "done". The file must close even if the write fails.
lessons.solution:
import os
proc writeAndClose() =
  let f = open("test.txt", fmWrite)
  defer: f.close()
  f.write("hello")
  echo "done"
writeAndClose()
lessons.hint: Wrap the code in a proc. `let f = open(...)`, then `defer: f.close()` on the next line.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Go's `defer`, Zig's `defer`. Python uses `with` for resource management. Nim's `defer` is closest to Go's.

lessons.name: Result Type Pattern
lessons.task:
Implement a function `readFileContent` that reads the content of a file and returns it as a string wrapped in a `Result` type. If the file does not exist, return an error message. The function should have the following signature:

```nim
proc readFileContent(filename: string): Result[string]
```

Expected behavior:
- If the file exists, return `Ok(content)` where `content` is the file's content.
- If the file does not exist, return `Err("File not found: " & filename)`.

Example usage:

```nim
let result = readFileContent("example.txt")
case result:
  of Ok(content):

  of Err(msg):

```

lessons.name: Spawn Intro
lessons.concept:
Nim has powerful and easy-to-use concurrency features built into the language.
The `spawn` keyword can run a procedure in a separate thread.
This is a deep topic, so we're just scratching the surface here. The main takeaway is that concurrency in Nim is a first-class citizen.

lessons.name: FlowVar
lessons.concept:
Running a `proc` in another thread is cool, but how do you get a result back?
A `FlowVar[T]` is a special type that acts as a container for a value that will be computed concurrently.
You can `^` to wait for the result and retrieve it.
lessons.task: Import `threadpool`. Write a `proc` that returns `42`. `spawn` it into a `FlowVar[int]` and `echo` the result.

var result = spawn calc()
echo ^result
