lessons.name: Creating a Nimble Package
lessons.concept:
Shipping code means packaging it properly. Nimble is Nim's build tool and package manager.
A `.nimble` file defines your package. Required fields: `version`, `author`, `description`, `license`. You can also set `srcDir` for source directories, `bin` for executable names, and `requires` for dependencies.
The format is a Nim-based DSL — it looks like Nim code because it IS Nim code, evaluated at build time. `nimble init` scaffolds this for you, but understanding the structure means you can write it by hand when you need something specific.
lessons.task: Write a proc `generateNimbleFile(name, desc: string): string` that returns a minimal nimble file content string with version "0.1.0", author "You", license "MIT", srcDir "src", and the given name/description. Echo the result for name="mycli" and desc="A CLI tool".
lessons.solution:
import std/strformat

proc generateNimbleFile(name, desc: string): string =
  fmt"""# Package

version       = "0.1.0"
author        = "You"
description   = "{desc}"
license       = "MIT"
srcDir        = "src"
bin           = @["{name}"]

requires "nim >= 1.6.0"
"""

echo generateNimbleFile("mycli", "A CLI tool")
lessons.hint: Use `fmt` from `std/strformat` with a triple-quoted template string. Include `version`, `author`, `description`, `license`, `srcDir`, `bin`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: `setup.py` / `pyproject.toml`. Rust: `Cargo.toml`. Nim's flat key-value format is the simplest — no TOML parsing, no complex AST.

docs.title: Nimble - Package Manager
docs.summary: Nim's package manager: creating, publishing, and installing Nim packages.

lessons.name: Documentation Generation
lessons.concept:
Code without docs is just a puzzle for the next person (who might be you in six months).
`nim doc` generates HTML documentation from your source code. Doc comments start with `##` (two hashes). You can use markdown-like formatting, code blocks with \`\`\`nim, and pragmas like `{.deprecated.}` and `{.since.}`.
Generated docs include the proc signature, doc comment body, and any examples. This is non-negotiable for any package you plan to publish. The Nim documentation toolchain is one of the best in the compiled-language world — comparable to Rustdoc.
lessons.task: Write a proc `nimDocStyle(procName, signature: string): string` that wraps a proc signature in proper doc comments with a description and a code example. Echo the result for procName="greet" and signature="greet(name: string)".
lessons.solution:
import std/strformat

proc nimDocStyle(procName, signature: string): string =
  fmt"""## {procName}
##
## Does something useful with {procName}.
##
## Example:
## ```nim
## let result = {procName} {signature}
## ```
proc {signature} = discard"""

echo nimDocStyle("greet", "greet(name: string)")
lessons.hint: Use `##` for doc comments, ` ```nim ` for code examples. Use `fmt` to interpolate `procName` and `signature`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: `'''` docstrings. Rust: `///`. Nim's `##` is closest to Rust — both generate HTML docs from comments directly above declarations.

docs.title: Docgen
docs.summary: Generating documentation from Nim source code comments.

lessons.name: Release Workflow
lessons.concept:
You've built something. Now release it properly.
Semantic versioning: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. Bump MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for new features, PATCH for bug fixes.
`nimble publish` sends your package to the Nim package directory. Before that: update CHANGELOG, bump version in `.nimble`, and tag the Git commit.
This lesson shows you how to automate version bumps. The pattern is simple: split on dots, parse the pieces, increment the right one, and zero out anything to the right. It's the kind of utility function every project ends up writing.
lessons.task: Write a proc `bumpVersion(current: string; part: string): string` that increments a semver string: pass "major", "minor", or "patch" for the part to bump. Echo the result of bumping version "0.9.9" as a major bump.
lessons.solution:
import std/strutils, std/strformat

proc bumpVersion(current: string; part: string): string =
  let parts = current.split(".")
  var mj = parts[0].parseInt
  var mn = parts[1].parseInt
  var pt = parts[2].parseInt
  case part
    of "major": mj += 1; mn = 0; pt = 0
    of "minor": mn += 1; pt = 0
    of "patch": pt += 1
    else: discard
  fmt"{mj}.{mn}.{pt}"
echo bumpVersion("0.9.9", "major")
lessons.hint: Parse with `current.split(".")`, parse each part with `.parseInt`, case on bump type, zero out lower parts. Use `fmt"{mj}.{mn}.{pt}"` to reassemble.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: `semver.bump_minor('0.9.9')` from the semver package. Rust: needs an external crate like `semver`. Nim: implement it yourself in < 15 lines of stdlib code.

docs.title: Nimble - Package Manager
docs.summary: Nim's package manager: creating, publishing, and installing Nim packages.

lessons.name: CI/CD for Nim projects
lessons.concept: CI/CD, or Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment, is a practice where you automate the integration of code changes from multiple contributors into a shared repository, followed by automated deployment. In this lesson, you'll set up a GitHub Actions workflow to automatically build and test your Nim project whenever changes are pushed to the repository. This ensures that your project remains stable and free of errors. Here's a quick rundown: you'll create a `.github/workflows` directory in your repository, and inside it, a YAML file that defines the workflow. The workflow will use the `actions/setup-nim` action to set up the Nim compiler, and then run your tests using the `nim test` command. It's like having a robot do your testing for you, so you can focus on writing code instead of fixing broken builds.
lessons.task: Create a GitHub Actions workflow file named `.github/workflows/nim.yml` in your Nimlings repository. The workflow should set up the Nim compiler and run the tests in your project. The `nim test` command should be used to run the tests. Ensure that the workflow runs on pushes to the `main` branch. Here's an example of what the workflow file might look like:
lessons.solution:
name: Nim CI
on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v2
    - name: Set up Nim
      uses: actions/setup-nim@v1
      with:
        version: '1.6.0'
    - name: Run tests
      run: nim test
lessons.hint: Remember, the key to a successful CI/CD setup is automation. Don't try to manually test every commit; let the robot do it for you. Just make sure your tests are comprehensive enough to catch any issues.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: GitHub Actions with Python projects typically use `actions/setup-python` and `pytest` for testing. Rust equivalent: GitHub Actions with Rust projects use `actions-rs/toolchain` and `cargo test` for testing. C++ equivalent: GitHub Actions with C++ projects use `actions/setup-compiler` and `cmake` for building and testing.

docs.summary: Nim's package manager: creating, publishing, and installing Nim packages.

lessons.name: NimScript
lessons.concept:
NimScript is a subset of Nim that can be evaluated by Nim's built-in virtual machine. It's primarily used as a build tool, allowing you to define tasks like `build`, `tests`, and `bench`. The `task` template from the `system` module lets you create these tasks. For instance, defining a `build` task is as simple as setting the command to `c`.

Here's a quick example:

```nim
import system

task build, "builds an example":
  setCommand "c"
```

This script, when executed, will compile your Nim program using the C backend. It's a powerful tool for automating your build process, much like Makefiles in other languages.
lessons.task:
Create a NimScript build script named `build.nims` that defines a `build` task to compile a Nim program. Your script should compile a program named `solution.nim`.

Expected output when running `nim build.nims build`:
```
Hint: used config file '/path/to/build.nims'
Hint: system [Processing]
Hint: solution [Processing]
Hint: solution [Done]
Hint:  [Linking]
Hint:  [Done]
```

Make sure your script is in the same directory as `solution.nim`.
lessons.solution:
import system

task build, "builds the solution":
  setCommand "c"
  setTarget "solution"
lessons.hint: Remember, you need to set the target to `solution` to ensure the correct file is compiled. Also, make sure your script is named `build.nims` and is in the same directory as `solution.nim`.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Using Makefile or a custom script. Rust equivalent: Using Cargo.toml and Cargo commands. C++ equivalent: Using Makefile or a custom script.

docs.title: NimScript
docs.summary: Nim as a scripting language with .nims build files.

lessons.name: nimsuggest
lessons.concept: Nimsuggest is a powerful tool that allows IDEs to query a Nim source file for useful information like symbol definitions. It's a frontend for the Nim compiler, making it fast and efficient. To use nimsuggest, you first need to run it with the `--stdin` option, which makes it read queries from standard input. A query consists of a command like `def` for definitions, followed by the file name, line, and column. For example, `def myfile.nim 10 5` would query the definition of the symbol at line 10, column 5 in `myfile.nim`. The output includes the type, source file, line/column information, and possibly a docstring. This is crucial for features like 'Jump to Definition' in IDEs.
lessons.task: Write a Nim program that uses nimsuggest to find the definition of a symbol. Your program should take a file name, line, and column as input, and then query nimsuggest for the definition of the symbol at that location. The output should include the type, source file, line, and column of the definition. For example, if you query the definition of a proc named `add` at line 10, column 5 in `example.nim`, the output should be something like `proc add(a, b: int): int defined in example.nim at line 10, column 5`.
lessons.solution:
import os, strutils, process

def get_definition(file: string, line: int, col: int): string:
  let nimsuggest_cmd = "nimsuggest --stdin"
  let query = "def {file} {line} {col}"
  let result = execProcess(nimsuggest_cmd, input = query)
  return result.stdout

let file = "example.nim"
let line = 10
let col = 5
let definition = get_definition(file, line, col)
echo definition
lessons.hint: Remember, nimsuggest is your friend. Just give it the right command and location, and it'll spit out the definition like a champ. Don't forget to check the output format; it's all in the docs.
lessons.validation_code: "proc add(a, b: int): int defined in example.nim at line 10, column 5" in result.stdout
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Using a language server like Pyright to find symbol definitions. Rust equivalent: Using Rust Analyzer for similar functionality. C++ equivalent: Using Clang's libclang for code analysis and symbol resolution.

docs.title: Nimsuggest
docs.summary: LSP server and IDE integration for Nim: autocomplete, goto-def, hints.

lessons.name: choosenim Version Manager
lessons.concept: choosenim is a version manager for Nim, allowing you to easily switch between different versions of the Nim compiler. This is particularly useful for managing multiple projects with different Nim version requirements. To use choosenim, you first need to install it. Once installed, you can list available Nim versions and switch between them as needed. For example, to list all available versions, you would run `choosenim list`. To switch to a specific version, say `1.4.0`, you would run `choosenim 1.4.0`. This ensures that your projects are using the correct Nim version without conflicts.
lessons.task: Install choosenim on your system and use it to list available Nim versions. Then, switch to a specific version, such as `1.4.0`. Verify that you can list the versions and switch between them successfully.
lessons.solution:
The solution involves installing choosenim and using its commands to list and switch Nim versions. Here is a step-by-step guide:

1. **Install choosenim**:
   ```sh
   sh <(curl -sL https://nim-lang.org/choosenim/init.sh)
   ```

2. **List available Nim versions**:
   ```sh
   choosenim list
   ```

3. **Switch to a specific Nim version** (e.g., `1.4.0`):
   ```sh
   choosenim 1.4.0
   ```

After completing these steps, you should be able to see the list of Nim versions and switch between them using choosenim.
lessons.hint: Remember, the key is to use `choosenim list` to see all available versions and `choosenim <version>` to switch. Don't forget to check the output to ensure the switch was successful.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: `pyenv` for managing Python versions. Rust equivalent: `rustup` for managing Rust versions. C++ equivalent: `nvm` for managing Node.js versions, though C++ itself doesn't have a version manager like choosenim.

docs.title: Choosenim
docs.summary: Nim version manager for installing and switching between Nim releases.

lessons.name: nim check (static analysis)
lessons.concept: In Nim, type classes are used to match types in the context of overload resolution or the `is` operator. For example, to check if a value is an object, you can use the `object` type class. Here's a quick example: `let x: object = MyObject(); echo x is object; # prints true`. Similarly, you can create custom type classes to match more complex types. For instance, `type RecordType = (tuple or object);` will match any tuple or object. This is useful for writing generic functions that can operate on different types as long as they meet certain criteria.
lessons.task: Implement a generic function `isTypeClass` that takes a value and a type class as parameters and returns `true` if the value is of the specified type class, and `false` otherwise. For example, `isTypeClass(42, int)` should return `true`, and `isTypeClass("hello", float)` should return `false`. Your function should be able to handle any type class, including built-in ones like `object`, `tuple`, `enum`, etc.
lessons.hint: Remember, the `is` operator in Nim is your friend here. Just make sure you're passing the correct type class to your function. Also, don't forget to import `typetraits` if you need any type-related utilities.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, type checking is done using isinstance(). In Rust, type checking is done using type annotations and pattern matching. In C++, type checking is done using dynamic_cast and typeid.

docs.title: Nim Compiler User Guide
docs.summary: Compiler flags, optimization, cross-compilation, and build configuration.

lessons.name: Hot Code Reloading
lessons.concept: Hot code reloading (HCR) in Nim allows you to apply changes to a running program without restarting it. This is particularly useful during development. HCR works by reloading individual modules. When a module is reloaded, new global variables are initialized, but existing state is preserved. To use HCR, you need to compile your program with the `--hotcodereloading:on` flag. The main module is non-reloadable, so you should put your logic in a separate module. Use `performCodeReload()` to trigger a reload. This feature is based on dynamic library hot swapping for native targets and direct manipulation of the global namespace for JavaScript targets.
lessons.task: Implement hot code reloading in a simple Nim program that changes the color of a bouncing rectangle on F9 key press. Your program should use SDL2 for rendering. The main logic should be in a separate module called `logic.nim`. When F9 is pressed, the color of the rectangle should change without restarting the program.
lessons.solution:
# logic.nim
import sdl2
import std / hotcodereloading

var runGame *: bool = true
var window: WindowPtr
var renderer: RendererPtr
var evt = sdl2.defaultEvent

proc init*() =
  discard sdl2.init(INIT_EVERYTHING)
  window = createWindow("testing", SDL_WINDOWPOS_UNDEFINED.cint, SDL_WINDOWPOS_UNDEFINED.cint, 640, 480, 0'u32)
  assert(window != nil, $sdl2.getError())
  renderer = createRenderer(window, -1, RENDERER_SOFTWARE)
  assert(renderer != nil, $sdl2.getError())

proc destroy*() =
  destroyRenderer(renderer)
  destroyWindow(window)

var posX: cint = 1
var posY: cint = 0
var dX: cint = 1
var dY: cint = 1
var color: (cint, cint, cint) = (255, 128, 128)

proc update*() =
  while pollEvent(evt):
    if evt.kind == QuitEvent:
      runGame = false
      break
    if evt.kind == KeyDown:
      if evt.key.keysym.scancode == SDL_SCANCODE_ESCAPE:
        runGame = false
      elif evt.key.keysym.scancode == SDL_SCANCODE_F9:
        performCodeReload()

  posX += dX
  posY += dY
  if posX >= 640:
    dX = -2
  if posX <= 0:
    dX = +2
  if posY >= 480:
    dY = -2
  if posY <= 0:
    dY = +2

  discard renderer.setDrawColor(0, 0, 255, 255)
  discard renderer.clear()
  discard renderer.setDrawColor(color[0], color[1], color[2], 0)
  var rect: Rect = (x: posX - 25, y: posY - 25, w: 50.cint, h: 50.cint)
  discard renderer.fillRect(rect)
  delay(16)
  renderer.present()

# mymain.nim
import logic

proc main() =
  init()
  while runGame:
    update()
  destroy()

main()
lessons.hint: Remember, you need to compile your program with `--hotcodereloading:on` and keep the main program running. Change the color in `logic.nim` and recompile without restarting the main program. The rectangle's color should change when F9 is pressed.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Live reloading with tools like Flask's reloader or Django's development server. Rust equivalent: Hot reloading with tools like Cargo Watch. C++ equivalent: Hot reloading with tools like LiveReload or Reloader++.

docs.title: Hot Code Reloading
docs.summary: Replacing code at runtime without restarting the application.

lessons.name: No GC Mode ({.noGC.})
lessons.concept:
In Nim, the `{.noGC.}` pragma allows you to disable the garbage collector for specific blocks of code. This is useful when you need precise control over memory allocation and deallocation, similar to languages like C or C++. However, it comes with the responsibility of manually managing memory, which can lead to memory leaks if not handled properly. Here's a quick example:

```nim
{.noGC.}
var p = alloc0(100)  # Allocate 100 bytes of memory
# Use the memory...
dealloc(p)  # Don't forget to free it!
```

Remember, if you allocate memory for objects that contain garbage-collected types (like strings or sequences), you must reset them before deallocating the memory to avoid memory leaks.
lessons.task: Write a Nim program that allocates memory for a custom data structure with a string field using `{.noGC.}`. Ensure that you manually free the allocated memory and reset the string field before doing so. The program should print 'Memory freed successfully!' upon successful execution.
lessons.solution:
import std/os

proc main() {.noGC.} =
  type
    MyData = tuple[x, y: int, s: string]

  var data = cast[ptr MyData](alloc0(sizeof(MyData)))
  data.s = "Hello, Nim!"
  echo data.s

  # Reset the string field before deallocating
  reset(data.s)
  dealloc(data)
  echo "Memory freed successfully!"

main()
lessons.hint: Don't forget to reset the string field before deallocating the memory. Otherwise, you'll end up with a memory leak, and that's not cool at all.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In C++, manual memory management is done using `new` and `delete`. In Rust, it's handled with `Box` and `drop`. In Python, memory management is automatic and not manually controlled.

docs.title: Manual - NoGC Pragma
docs.summary: Disabling the garbage collector for real-time or embedded systems.

lessons.name: Memory-Mapped Files
lessons.concept:
Memory-mapped files are a way to map a file directly into the memory space of a process. This allows you to read and write to the file as if it were an array, which can be more efficient than traditional file I/O operations. In Nim, you can use the `memfiles` module to work with memory-mapped files. Here's a quick example:

```nim
import memfiles

let
  filePath = "example.txt"
  file = openFile(filePath)
  mmf = newMemoryMappedFile(file)
  data = mmf.read(0, mmf.size)

echo data
```

This code opens a file, creates a memory-mapped file object, reads the entire file content, and prints it. Note that you need to handle file opening and closing properly to avoid resource leaks.
lessons.task:
Write a Nim program that reads a file using memory-mapped files and prints its contents. The program should take the file path as a command-line argument and print the contents of the file. Assume the file exists and is readable.

**Expected Output:**
If the file `example.txt` contains the text `Hello, World!`, the program should output:
```
Hello, World!
```
lessons.hint: Remember, the key to memory-mapped files is to map the file into memory and then read from that memory. Don't forget to close the file after you're done!
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: `mmap` module / Rust equivalent: `mmap` crate / C++ equivalent: `mmap` system call

docs.title: std/memfiles
docs.summary: Memory-mapped file I/O for efficient large file access.

lessons.name: Inline Assembly
lessons.concept: Inline assembly in Nim allows you to embed low-level assembly code directly into your Nim programs. This can be useful for performance-critical sections or when interfacing with hardware. However, it's important to note that using inline assembly can make your code less portable and harder to maintain. Here's a simple example of adding two integers using inline assembly. The `asm` statement is used to embed the assembly code, and identifiers in the assembly code that refer to Nim identifiers are enclosed in backticks (`). For instance, to add two integers `a` and `b`, you might use `mov eax, `a`` and `add eax, `b``. The result is stored in the `result` variable, which is specified in the output constraint of the assembly code.
lessons.task: Write a Nim program that uses inline assembly to add two integers. The program should define a procedure `addInt` that takes two integers as parameters and returns their sum using inline assembly. The main function should call `addInt` with two integers and print the result. The expected output should be the sum of the two integers.
lessons.solution:
import std/io

proc addInt(a, b: int): int =
  asm ""
    mov eax, `a`
    add eax, `b`
    mov `result`, eax
  ""

var x = 5
var y = 7
var sum = addInt(x, y)
echo sum
lessons.hint: Remember, inline assembly is like using a chainsaw to cut butter. It's powerful but can be dangerous if not used properly. Make sure you're moving the right registers and setting the correct constraints.
lessons.cross_language_notes:
Python equivalent: Using ctypes to call a C function with inline assembly.
Rust equivalent: Using unsafe blocks to include inline assembly.
C++ equivalent: Directly using inline assembly within a function.

docs.title: Manual - Inline Assembly
docs.summary: Writing inline assembly for architecture-specific operations.

lessons.name: Interrupt Handlers
lessons.concept:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to handle keyboard interrupts in Nim using the `signals` module. When you press Ctrl+C, your program should catch the interrupt and execute a custom handler. This is useful for cleaning up resources or gracefully shutting down your application. Here's a simple example:

```nim
import signals

proc handleInterrupt(signum: int) {.raises: [], tags: [].} =
  echo "Interrupt received!"
  exit(0)

signals.setSignalHandler(signals.SIGINT, handleInterrupt)

echo "Press Ctrl+C to interrupt..."
while true:
  sleep(1000)
```

In this example, the `handleInterrupt` procedure is defined to print a message and exit the program when a SIGINT signal is received. The `signals.setSignalHandler` function is used to register this handler for the SIGINT signal.
lessons.task: Write a Nim program that catches a keyboard interrupt (SIGINT) and prints 'Interrupt received!' when the user presses Ctrl+C. Your program should continuously run until interrupted.
lessons.solution:
import signals

proc handleInterrupt(signum: int) {.raises: [], tags: [].} =
  echo "Interrupt received!"
  exit(0)

signals.setSignalHandler(signals.SIGINT, handleInterrupt)

echo "Press Ctrl+C to interrupt..."
while true:
  sleep(1000)
lessons.hint: Remember to import the `signals` module and use `signals.setSignalHandler` to register your interrupt handler. Don't forget to include an infinite loop to keep the program running.
lessons.cross_language_notes:
Python equivalent: Use the `signal` module to set a signal handler for `signal.SIGINT`.
Rust equivalent: Use the `signal_hook` crate to register a signal handler for `SIGINT`.
C++ equivalent: Use the `signal` function from the C standard library to set a signal handler for `SIGINT`.

lessons.name: SIMD Intrinsics
lessons.concept:
SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) intrinsics allow you to perform the same operation on multiple data points simultaneously, which can significantly speed up computations. However, they require careful handling to ensure data consistency and prevent data races. In this lesson, we'll focus on using locks and guards to prevent data races in a concurrent program. Remember, in the real world, you'd never write a program like this, but it's good to know how to handle concurrency properly.

Here's a simple example of using a lock and a guard in Nim:

```nim
import locks

var sharedData {.guard.} = 0
var lock = createLock()

proc increment() =
  lock.lock()
  defer: lock.unlock()
  sharedData += 1

# Example usage
increment()
```
lessons.task: Write a Nim program that increments a shared variable `counter` by 1000 in 10 threads. Use locks and guards to prevent data races. The final value of `counter` should be 10000. Ensure that your program runs without data races and produces the correct output.
lessons.hint: Remember to lock the shared resource before modifying it and unlock it afterward. Use the `defer` statement to ensure the lock is always released, even if an error occurs.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, you can use the `threading` module with locks. In Rust, you can use `std::sync::Mutex`. In C++, you can use `std::mutex` from the `<mutex>` header.

lessons.name: SQLite Integration
lessons.concept:
SQLite is a lightweight, disk-based database that doesn't require a separate server process. In Nim, you can use the `db_sqlite` module to interact with SQLite databases. Here's a quick example of how to create a table, insert data, and retrieve it:

```nim
import db_sqlite

var db = open("test.db")
db.exec(sql"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT)")
db.exec(sql"INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice')")

for row in db.queryRows(sql"SELECT * FROM users"):
  echo row
```

This code snippet creates a database file named `test.db`, creates a table named `users`, inserts a user named 'Alice', and then retrieves and prints all users from the table.
lessons.task: Write a Nim program that connects to an SQLite database, creates a table named `tasks`, inserts a task with the name 'Write Nim code', and retrieves and prints all tasks from the table. The program should create a database file named `tasks.db`.
lessons.hint: Remember, the key to success is not in the complexity of your code, but in its simplicity. Don't overthink it.
lessons.validation_code: result.exitCode == 0 and "Write Nim code" in result.stdout
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Use SQLite3 library; Rust equivalent: Use rusqlite crate; C++ equivalent: Use SQLite C API

lessons.name: Async Database Access
lessons.concept:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to perform asynchronous database operations in Nim using the `asyncdispatch` and `db_sqlite` libraries. Unlike synchronous operations that block the execution, asynchronous operations allow your program to perform other tasks while waiting for the database response. This is particularly useful for I/O-bound tasks like database access. Here's a quick example of how you might set up an asynchronous connection to an SQLite database and execute a simple query:

```nim
import asyncdispatch, db_sqlite

async proc queryDatabase(): Future[void] =
  let db = open("test.db")
  let result = db.exec(sql"SELECT * FROM users")
  for row in result:
    echo row
  db.close()

waitFor queryDatabase()
```

In this example, `open` is used to connect to the database, and `exec` is used to execute a SQL query. The `waitFor` function is used to wait for the asynchronous operation to complete. Note how the database operations are wrapped in an `async` procedure, making them non-blocking.
lessons.task: Your task is to write a Nim program that connects to an SQLite database named `nimlings.db`, executes a query to retrieve all records from a table named `users`, and prints each record. The program should use the `asyncdispatch` and `db_sqlite` libraries to perform the operations asynchronously. The expected output should be the records from the `users` table, each on a new line.
lessons.hint: Remember, the key to asynchronous operations is to wrap your database calls in an `async` procedure. Use `waitFor` to ensure your program waits for the async operation to complete before exiting.
lessons.validation_code: result.exitCode == 0 and "SELECT * FROM users" in result.stdout
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: asyncio with sqlite3; Rust equivalent: tokio with rusqlite; C++ equivalent: Boost.Asio with SQLite.

lessons.name: Working with CSV
lessons.concept:
CSV files are a common way to store tabular data. In Nim, you can use the `std/csv` module to read and parse CSV files. Here's a quick example of how to read a CSV file and print each row:

```nim
import csv

let reader = open("data.csv")
for row in reader.byRow():
  echo row
```

This code snippet opens a CSV file named `data.csv` and iterates over each row, printing it out. The `byRow` method of the `reader` object returns an iterator over the rows in the CSV file.
lessons.task:
Write a Nim program that reads a CSV file named `sample.csv`, parses it, and prints each row on a new line. The `sample.csv` file should contain the following content:

```
name,age,city
Alice,30,New York
Bob,25,Los Angeles
Charlie,35,Chicago
```

Your program should output:

```
name,age,city
Alice,30,New York
Bob,25,Los Angeles
Charlie,35,Chicago
```

**Note**: Ensure that your program handles potential file reading errors gracefully.
lessons.hint: Don't forget to handle file opening errors! Use a `try` block to catch any `IOError` that might occur when opening the file. Also, make sure to close the file after you're done reading from it.
lessons.validation_code: result.exitCode == 0 and "name,age,city" in result.stdout and "Alice,30,New York" in result.stdout and "Bob,25,Los Angeles" in result.stdout and "Charlie,35,Chicago" in result.stdout

lessons.name: Working with YAML/TOML
lessons.concept:
YAML and TOML are popular configuration file formats. In Nim, you can use the `nimyaml` library to parse YAML files. It's like when you finally learn to read a map, but instead of treasure, you get configuration data. Here's a quick example of how to parse a YAML file:

```nim
import nimyaml

let yamlStr = "name: John\nage: 30"
let yamlData = parseYaml(yamlStr)

echo yamlData.yamlGet("name").getStr()  # Output: John
echo yamlData.yamlGet("age").getInt()   # Output: 30
```

Notice how `parseYaml` turns the YAML string into a `YamlNode` object, which you can then query with `yamlGet`. It's like having a magical decoder ring for configuration files.
lessons.task:
Write a Nim program that reads a YAML configuration file named `config.yaml` and prints its contents. The `config.yaml` file should contain the following:

```yaml
name: Alice
age: 25
```

Your program should output:

```
name: Alice
age: 25
```

Make sure to use the `nimyaml` library to parse the YAML file.
lessons.hint: Remember, the key is to use `parseFile` from the `nimyaml` library to read the YAML file. Then, iterate over the pairs in the resulting `YamlNode` object to print each key-value pair. It's like opening a treasure chest and listing all the loot inside.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: Use PyYAML library. Rust: Use serde YAML crate. C++: Use yaml-cpp library.

lessons.name: nimgl / OpenGL Bindings
lessons.concept:
Concepts in Nim allow you to define custom type classes that match other types based on a given set of bindings. For instance, you can define a `Comparable` concept that requires a `cmp` procedure. Similarly, you can define an `Indexable` concept that requires `[]`, `[]=` and `len` procedures. This is useful for generic programming and overload resolution. Here's a simple example of a `Comparable` concept:

```nim
type
  Comparable = concept
    proc cmp(a, b: Self): int
```

This concept matches any type that has a `cmp` procedure. The `Self` keyword refers to the type being matched. Concepts are not concrete types themselves, but rather type classes that can be used in generic contexts.
lessons.task:
Create a Nim program that defines a custom type class for a simple 2D vector and implements basic operations like addition and comparison. Your program should include the following:

1. A `Vector2D` type with `x` and `y` fields.
2. A `Vector2D` concept that requires `add` and `cmp` procedures.
3. Implement the `add` and `cmp` procedures for the `Vector2D` type.
4. Write a `main` procedure that creates two `Vector2D` instances, adds them, and compares the result with another `Vector2D` instance. Print the results of the addition and comparison.

Expected output:
```
Added Vector: (3, 5)
Comparison: true
```
lessons.solution:
import strformat

type
  Vector2D = object
    x, y: float

  Vector2DConcept = concept
    proc add(a, b: Self): Self
    proc cmp(a, b: Self): int

proc add(a, b: Vector2D): Vector2D =
  return Vector2D(x: a.x + b.x, y: a.y + b.y)

proc cmp(a, b: Vector2D): int =
  if a.x == b.x and a.y == b.y:
    return 0
  elif a.x < b.x or (a.x == b.x and a.y < b.y):
    return -1
  else:
    return 1

proc `==`(a, b: Vector2D): bool =
  return cmp(a, b) == 0

proc `$`(v: Vector2D): string =
  return fmt"({v.x}, {v.y})"

proc main() =
  let v1 = Vector2D(x: 1, y: 2)
  let v2 = Vector2D(x: 2, y: 3)
  let v3 = Vector2D(x: 3, y: 5)

  let addedVector = add(v1, v2)
  echo "Added Vector: ", addedVector

  let comparison = addedVector == v3
  echo "Comparison: ", comparison

main()
lessons.hint: Don't forget to implement the `add` and `cmp` procedures for the `Vector2D` type. The `cmp` procedure should return 0 if the vectors are equal, -1 if the first vector is less than the second, and 1 otherwise. The `==` operator can be implemented using the `cmp` procedure.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, similar functionality can be achieved using classes and methods. In Rust, traits can be used to define custom type classes. In C++, concepts were introduced in C++20 and can be used to define custom type classes.

lessons.name: Terminal UI (illwill)
lessons.concept:
Alright, you've made it to the part where you're going to learn how to interact with the terminal in Nim. It's like when you're playing a game and you need to talk to the game, but instead of a game, it's a computer. You'll learn how to ask for the user's name and then greet them back. It's like being a social butterfly, but with a computer. Just remember, the computer doesn't care if you're shy or not, it just wants to know your name.

Here's a little snippet to get you started:
```nim
import os
let name = readLine(stdin)
echo "Hello, " & name & "!"
```
This code does three things: it imports the `os` module, which is necessary for reading from the terminal; it reads a line from the terminal (that's the user's name); and it echoes a greeting message back to the terminal. Easy peasy, right?
lessons.task:
Write a Nim program that does the following:
1. Prompts the user to enter their name.
2. Reads the user's name from the terminal.
3. Displays a greeting message that includes the user's name.

Expected output:
```
Enter your name: John
Hello, John!
```
lessons.solution:
import os

echo "Enter your name: "
let name = readLine(stdin)
echo "Hello, " & name & "!"
lessons.hint: Remember, the computer is just a tool. You're the one doing the work. So, if you're having trouble, just take a deep breath, and think about what you need to do. You need to ask for the user's name, read it, and then say hello. It's like a game, and you're the player. Just remember to have fun!
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, this can be achieved using the `input()` function to read user input and `print()` to display messages. In Rust, the `std::io` module is used for input and output operations. In C++, `std::cin` and `std::cout` are used for reading and writing to the terminal.

lessons.name: Fidget UI Framework
lessons.concept:
Hey there, Nimling! You're about to dive into the Fidget UI framework, a lightweight and easy-to-use library for creating GUI applications in Nim. Fidget allows you to build simple interfaces with minimal effort. In this lesson, you'll create a window with a button. When the button is clicked, the window will close. It's like magic, but it's all Nim. Here's a quick example of what you'll be doing:

```nim
import fidget

proc onButtonClick(event: ButtonClickEvent) {.async.} =
  event.window.close()

var app = newApp()
var window = newWindow(app, "My Window")
var button = newButton(window, "Close")
button.onClick = onButtonClick

app.run()
```

See how `onButtonClick` is defined with the `async` keyword? That's because Fidget uses async/await for handling events. When the button is clicked, `onButtonClick` is called, and the window is closed. Easy peasy!
lessons.task:
Your task is to create a simple GUI application using the Fidget UI framework. The application should display a window with a button. When the button is clicked, the window should close. Here's what you need to do:

1. Import the `fidget` module.
2. Define an `onButtonClick` procedure that takes a `ButtonClickEvent` and closes the window.
3. Create a new application, window, and button.
4. Assign the `onButtonClick` procedure to the button's `onClick` event.
5. Run the application.

Remember, the window should open with a button. When you click the button, the window should close. It's like a disappearing act, but it's all Nim!
lessons.hint: Hey, you got this! Just remember to define the `onButtonClick` procedure with the `async` keyword and make sure it closes the window. Then, create the application, window, and button, and connect the button's `onClick` event to your procedure. Finally, run the application. It's like putting together a puzzle, but it's all Nim!
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, Tkinter or PyQt could be used to create a similar GUI. In Rust, using a library like gtk-rs would achieve the same. In C++, Qt or wxWidgets could be used for GUI development.

lessons.name: WASM Compilation
lessons.concept:
Compiling Nim to WebAssembly (WASM) is a game changer for web development. Unlike other languages, Nim doesn't require a separate toolchain for WASM compilation. You just need to use the `--backend:js` flag and the `--vm:wasm` flag. Here's a simple example:

```nim
proc greet(): string =
  return "Hello, WebAssembly!"

when isMainModule:
  echo greet()
```

This code defines a simple procedure `greet` that returns a string. When executed, it prints `Hello, WebAssembly!` to the console. To compile this to WASM, you'd use the following command:

```bash
nim js --vm:wasm solution.nim
```

This will generate two files: `solution.js` and `solution.wasm`. The `solution.js` file is a JavaScript wrapper that loads and runs the `solution.wasm` file. You can then include these files in an HTML file to run your Nim code in the browser.
lessons.task: Write a Nim program that defines a procedure `add` which takes two integers and returns their sum. Compile this program to WebAssembly and run it in a browser. The procedure should be called with the arguments `5` and `3`, and the result should be printed to the console.
lessons.solution:
proc add(a, b: int): int =
  return a + b

when isMainModule:
  echo add(5, 3)
lessons.hint: Remember, you need to compile your Nim code to WebAssembly using the `--backend:js` and `--vm:wasm` flags. Once compiled, you'll have a `.js` and a `.wasm` file. Include these in an HTML file to run your code in the browser. Don't forget to check the console for the output.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Use Pyodide to compile Python code to WebAssembly. Rust equivalent: Use the wasm-pack toolchain to compile Rust code to WebAssembly. C++ equivalent: Use Emscripten to compile C++ code to WebAssembly.

lessons.name: Android Cross-Compilation
lessons.concept:
Cross-compiling Nim to Android targets requires the Android NDK and a special configuration.

The key steps are:
1. Install the Android NDK (via Android Studio or sdkmanager).
2. Create a `nim.cfg` or provide `--cpu:arm --os:android` with the correct NDK path.
3. Point `--gcc.path` to the NDK's standalone toolchain or use the nim-android package.

A minimal config:
```
# nim.cfg for Android ARM64
--cpu:arm64
--os:android
--gcc.path:PATH_TO_NDK/toolchains/llvm/prebuilt/linux-x86_64/bin
```

Common pitfalls: linking against the wrong libc, missing the `-llog` for Android logging, and needing `android: true` in your .nimble file for Java interop.
lessons.task: Describe the steps to cross-compile a Hello World Nim program for Android ARM64. What compiler flags, NDK paths, and config entries are needed? What are the most common build failures and how do you fix them?
lessons.hint: The key flags are `--os:android --cpu:arm64`. The NDK path points to the `bin/` directory containing `aarch64-linux-android21-clang`. Common errors: 'cannot find -lGL' (add `--noLinking`), 'unknown OS: android' (use nightly Nim), missing `-ldl` (linker script).
lessons.cross_language_notes: Rust: `rustup target add aarch64-linux-android`. Go: `GOOS=android GOARCH=arm64` -- drastically simpler. Nim's cross-compilation is more manual but gives you direct C interop with the NDK's native APIs.

lessons.name: iOS Cross-Compilation
lessons.concept:
iOS cross-compilation is a bit of a headache, but it's doable. You need to set up your Nim environment to target iOS. The key is to use the `--cpu:arm` and `--os:ios` flags. Here's a quick example of how you might compile a simple Nim program for iOS:

```nim
import os

```

This is about as simple as it gets. Just remember to have your iOS development environment set up correctly, including Xcode and the necessary SDKs.

lessons.hint: Don't forget the flags, `--cpu:arm` and `--os:ios`. And yes, you'll need Xcode and the iOS SDKs installed. It's a bit of a hassle, but it's worth it for that iOS feeling.

lessons.name: Embedded Nim (microcontrollers)
lessons.concept:
In Nim, methods for custom types must be defined in the same module where the type is declared. This is a bit different from other languages like Python or Rust, where methods can be defined outside the class or struct. For example, in Python, you can define a method outside the class, but in Nim, you can't define a method for a type in a different module unless you use the `base` pragma, which is a bit tricky and not recommended for beginners. Here's a quick example of a method defined within a type in Nim:

```nim
type
  Base* = ref object
    value: int

method foo(x: Base) {.base.} =
  echo "The value is: ", x.value
```

In this example, the method `foo` is defined within the same module as the type `Base`. This is the only way to ensure that the method is correctly scoped and can be called on instances of `Base`.
lessons.task: Define a method named `bar` for a custom type `Sensor` in the same module. The method should take no parameters and print the message `"Sensor is ready"`. Ensure that the method is correctly scoped to the `Sensor` type.
lessons.solution:
type
  Sensor* = ref object

method bar(x: Sensor) {.base.} =
  echo "Sensor is ready"
lessons.hint: Remember, you can't define a method for a type in a different module unless you use the `base` pragma. Since we're in the same module, just define the method right after the type declaration. It's like keeping your toys in the same box; they belong together!
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, methods are defined within class definitions and are inherently scoped to the class. In Rust, methods are defined within the impl block of a struct and are scoped to the struct. In C++, methods are defined within the class definition and are scoped to the class.

docs.title: Compiler - Cross-Compilation
docs.summary: Cross-compiling Nim to different CPU architectures and operating systems.

lessons.name: Dynamic Libraries & Plugins
lessons.concept:
In Nim, dynamic method resolution is achieved using the `method` keyword instead of `proc`. This allows methods in derived classes to override methods in base classes. Here's a simple example:

```nim
type
  Base = ref object of RootObj
  Derived = ref object of Base

method foo(a: Base) {.base.} =
  echo "base"

method foo(a: Derived) =
  echo "derived"
```

In this example, calling `foo` on an instance of `Derived` will output `derived` because the method in `Derived` overrides the one in `Base`.
lessons.task: Create a Nim program that defines a base class `Thing` with a method `m` that prints 'base'. Define a derived class `Unit` that overrides the method `m` to first call the base class's `m` method and then print '1'. When you call `m` on an instance of `Unit`, the output should be 'base' followed by '1'.
lessons.solution:
type
  Thing = ref object of RootObj
  Unit = ref object of Thing
    x: int

method m(a: Thing) {.base.} =
  echo "base"

method m(a: Unit) =
  procCall m(Thing(a))
  echo "1"

var u = Unit(x: 42)
m(u)
lessons.hint: Remember, to call the base class method from the derived class, use `procCall m(Thing(a))` before your custom logic. Don't forget to instantiate your `Unit` class and call the method on it.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, this is achieved using method overriding. In Rust, similar behavior can be implemented using trait inheritance. In C++, this is done using virtual functions.

docs.title: Manual - Dynamic Linking
docs.summary: Loading shared libraries at runtime with dlopen/LoadLibrary.

lessons.name: std/sha1 Hashing
lessons.concept:
Hashing is like giving your data a unique fingerprint. SHA-1 is one of those fingerprinting algorithms that generates a 160-bit (20-byte) hash value, typically rendered as a 40-digit hexadecimal number. It's like a digital signature for your data, ensuring that it hasn't been tampered with. In Nim, you can use the `std/sha1` module to generate SHA-1 hashes. Here's a quick example:

```nim
import sha1

echo sha1("hello world").hexdigest()
```

This will output the SHA-1 hash of the string "hello world". Note that SHA-1 is considered cryptographically broken and unsuitable for further use in security-sensitive applications, but it's still useful for non-cryptographic purposes like checksums.

**Task:** Implement a function that takes a string and returns its SHA-1 hash.
lessons.task: Write a function named `getSha1Hash` that takes a single string parameter and returns its SHA-1 hash as a hexadecimal string. For example, calling `getSha1Hash("hello world")` should return the string `"2ef7bde608ce5404e97d5f042f95f89f1c232871"`.
lessons.hint: Don't overthink it. Just remember that `sha1(input).hexdigest()` is your friend. And if you forget, just look at the example in the concept section. It's like they're telling you the answer in plain English.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: hashlib.sha1(input_string.encode()).hexdigest() / Rust: sha1::Sha1::from(input_string).hexdigest() / C++: std::string sha1(const std::string& input)

docs.title: std/sha1
docs.summary: SHA-1 hashing, HMAC support, and secure digest computation.

lessons.name: Secure Random Numbers
lessons.concept:
Generating secure random numbers is crucial for cryptographic applications and secure token generation. Unlike the `random` module, which is suitable for non-cryptographic purposes, the `sysrand` module provides a cryptographically secure pseudorandom number generator. This means the numbers generated are unpredictable and suitable for security-sensitive tasks. Here's a simple example of how to use `sysrand` to generate a secure random number:

```nim
import sysrand

let secureRandomNumber = sysrand.randInt(1, 100)
echo secureRandomNumber
```

In this example, `randInt` generates a random integer between 1 and 100 inclusive. The `sysrand` module ensures that the number is generated securely.
lessons.task: Write a Nim program that generates a secure random number between 1 and 100 and prints it. Use the `sysrand` library for this task.
lessons.hint: Remember, security is not just about making things work, it's about making them work in a way that can't be easily broken. Use `sysrand` for the job.

lessons.name: Constant-Time Comparison
lessons.concept:
Constant-time comparison is crucial in cryptographic applications to prevent timing attacks. A timing attack can exploit the time taken by a comparison function to infer information about the inputs. In Nim, you can implement a constant-time comparison function using bitwise operations to ensure that the time taken is independent of the input values. Here's a simple example:

```nim
proc constantTimeCompare(a, b: string): bool =
  if a.len != b.len:
    return false
  var result = 0
  for i in 0..<a.len:
    result = result or (ord(a[i]) xor ord(b[i]))
  return result == 0
```

This function first checks if the lengths of the strings are equal. If not, it immediately returns false. Then, it uses bitwise operations to compare each character of the strings without short-circuiting, ensuring the time taken is constant regardless of the input.
lessons.task: Implement a constant-time comparison function in Nim. The function should take two strings as input and return true if they are equal, and false otherwise. Ensure that the function does not short-circuit and takes constant time regardless of the input values.
lessons.solution:
proc constantTimeCompare(a, b: string): bool =
  if a.len != b.len:
    return false
  var result = 0
  for i in 0..<a.len:
    result = result or (ord(a[i]) xor ord(b[i]))
  return result == 0
lessons.hint: Remember, the key is to avoid short-circuiting and ensure the time taken is constant. Use bitwise operations to compare each character without revealing information through timing.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Use `hmac.compare_digest()`. Rust equivalent: Use `constant_time_eq::constant_time_eq()`. C++ equivalent: Use `TimingSafeComparison::compare()`. These languages provide built-in or third-party functions to ensure constant-time comparisons to prevent timing attacks.

lessons.name: Input Validation Patterns
lessons.concept:
Input validation patterns are crucial for security. Common patterns in Nim:

**1. Whitelist validation (best):** Only accept known-good inputs.
```nim
const ValidCommands = @["start", "stop", "status"]
if cmd notin ValidCommands: raise newException(ValueError, "invalid")
```

**2. Length bounding:** Reject excessively long inputs to prevent DOS.
```nim
if input.len > 1024: raise newException(ValueError, "too long")
```

**3. Format validation:** Use regex or manual parsing for structured values.
```nim
import std/strutils
if not input.contains(Regex(r'^[a-zA-Z0-9_]+$')): raise ...
```

**4. Boundary checks for numeric inputs:** Check ranges, not just types.

Combine these patterns -- never rely on a single validation layer. Always validate at the API boundary (before processing), not after half the work is done.
lessons.task: Explain the 'validate before use' principle and the four validation patterns (whitelist, length bounding, format validation, boundary checks). Write a Nim code example that validates a username: max 32 chars, alphanumeric only, first character must be a letter.
lessons.hint: Use `strutils.isAlphaNumeric` for char checks. Combine checks with early returns / exceptions. Remember that validation at input boundaries prevents injection attacks -- never call `exec` or `readFile` with unvalidated user input.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: similar whitelist/bound approach with `re.match`. Rust: type-driven -- use `String` vs `SanitizedString` newtype pattern. OWASP recommends the same four patterns across all languages.

lessons.name: Builder Pattern
lessons.concept:
The builder pattern is a design pattern used to construct complex objects step by step. Instead of using a single constructor with many parameters, the builder pattern uses multiple methods to set each field. This makes the code more readable and maintainable. For example, instead of creating a `Student` object with `Student(name: "Anton", age: 5)`, you can use a builder to set the `name` and `age` separately. Here's a simple example in Nim:

```nim
type
  StudentBuilder = object
    name: string
    age: int

  Student = object
    name: string
    age: int

proc setName(builder: var StudentBuilder, name: string) =
  builder.name = name

proc setAge(builder: var StudentBuilder, age: int) =
  builder.age = age

proc build(builder: StudentBuilder): Student =
  Student(name: builder.name, age: builder.age)

var builder = StudentBuilder()
setName(builder, "Anton")
setAge(builder, 5)
let student = build(builder)

echo student.name, " ", student.age
```
This code defines a `StudentBuilder` object with methods to set the `name` and `age`. The `build` method creates a `Student` object from the builder. The output of this code is `Anton 5`.
lessons.task: Implement a builder pattern for a complex object in Nim. The object should be called `Car` and should have the following fields: `make`, `model`, `year`, `color`, and `engineSize`. The builder should have methods to set each field. Finally, the builder should have a `build` method that returns a `Car` object. The `Car` object should be printed with its fields in the following format: `Car(make: <make>, model: <model>, year: <year>, color: <color>, engineSize: <engineSize>)`.
lessons.solution:
type
  CarBuilder = object
    make: string
    model: string
    year: int
    color: string
    engineSize: float

  Car = object
    make: string
    model: string
    year: int
    color: string
    engineSize: float

proc setMake(builder: var CarBuilder, make: string) =
  builder.make = make

proc setModel(builder: var CarBuilder, model: string) =
  builder.model = model

proc setYear(builder: var CarBuilder, year: int) =
  builder.year = year

proc setColor(builder: var CarBuilder, color: string) =
  builder.color = color

proc setEngineSize(builder: var CarBuilder, engineSize: float) =
  builder.engineSize = engineSize

proc build(builder: CarBuilder): Car =
  Car(make: builder.make, model: builder.model, year: builder.year, color: builder.color, engineSize: builder.engineSize)

var builder = CarBuilder()
setMake(builder, "Toyota")
setModel(builder, "Corolla")
setYear(builder, 2020)
setColor(builder, "Red")
setEngineSize(builder, 1.8)
let car = build(builder)

echo "Car(make: ", car.make, ", model: ", car.model, ", year: ", car.year, ", color: ", car.color, ", engineSize: ", car.engineSize, ")"
lessons.hint: Remember, the key to the builder pattern is breaking down the construction of the object into multiple steps. Don't try to set all the fields in one go. Instead, create methods for each field and use them to build the object step by step. And don't forget to implement the `build` method to return the final `Car` object.
lessons.validation_code: result.stdout.strip() == "Car(make: Toyota, model: Corolla, year: 2020, color: Red, engineSize: 1.8)"
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Use classes with methods to set each field. Rust equivalent: Use structs with builder methods. C++ equivalent: Use a class with a nested Builder class.

lessons.name: Strategy Pattern (proc types)
lessons.concept:
The Strategy Pattern is a behavioral design pattern that enables selecting an algorithm at runtime. In Nim, you can implement this pattern using proc types. Here's a cynical take: imagine you're building a game where the character can move in different ways, and you want to switch between these movement strategies dynamically. Instead of hardcoding each movement type, you can define a proc type for movement and then implement different strategies as procs that conform to this type. This way, you can easily switch between strategies without changing the core game logic.

For example, let's say you have a `move` proc type and two movement strategies: `walk` and `run`. You can define these strategies as procs and then use them interchangeably in your game. Here's a small code snippet to illustrate:

```nim
type
  Move = proc(x, y: int)

proc walk(x, y: int) =
  echo "Walking to (", x, ", ", y, ")"

proc run(x, y: int) =
  echo "Running to (", x, ", ", y, ")"

var movement: Move

# Set the movement strategy to walk
movement = walk
movement(10, 20)  # Output: Walking to (10, 20)

# Change the movement strategy to run
movement = run
movement(30, 40)  # Output: Running to (30, 40)
```

In this example, the `Move` proc type defines the signature for movement strategies, and `walk` and `run` are two concrete implementations. By assigning different procs to the `movement` variable, you can dynamically change the behavior of your game character's movement.
lessons.task:
Your task is to implement a simple game where a character can attack enemies using different strategies. The game should have an `attack` proc type and two attack strategies: `punch` and `kick`. The user should be able to choose the attack strategy at runtime. Implement the game so that it correctly applies the chosen strategy based on user input.

Expected output:
```
Choose an attack strategy (punch/kick): punch
Punching the enemy!

Choose an attack strategy (punch/kick): kick
Kicking the enemy!
```
lessons.solution:
import strutils

type
  Attack = proc()

proc punch() =
  echo "Punching the enemy!"

proc kick() =
  echo "Kicking the enemy!"

var attackStrategy: Attack
var userInput: string

# Get user input for the attack strategy
echo "Choose an attack strategy (punch/kick): "
readLine(inputStream, userInput)
userInput = userInput.strip().toLower()

# Set the attack strategy based on user input
if userInput == "punch":
  attackStrategy = punch
elif userInput == "kick":
  attackStrategy = kick
else:
  echo "Invalid strategy!"
  return

# Execute the chosen attack strategy
attackStrategy()
lessons.hint: Remember, the key is to define a proc type for the attack strategy and then implement different strategies as procs that conform to this type. Use user input to decide which strategy to use at runtime. Don't forget to handle invalid input gracefully.
lessons.validation_code:
result.exitCode == 0 and "Choose an attack strategy (punch/kick): punch
Punching the enemy!
Choose an attack strategy (punch/kick): kick
Kicking the enemy!" in result.stdout

lessons.name: Observer Pattern (signals)
lessons.concept:
The Observer Pattern is a design pattern where an object, called the subject, maintains a list of its dependents, called observers, and notifies them automatically of any state changes, usually by calling one of their methods. In Nim, this can be implemented using signals. Here's a simple example: 

```nim
import signals

var subject = newSubject[int]()

proc onSubjectChange(value: int) =
  echo "Subject changed to: ", value

subject.connect(onSubjectChange)

subject.send(42)  # Output: Subject changed to: 42
```

In this example, `subject` is an object that can send signals. `onSubjectChange` is a procedure that gets called whenever `subject` sends a signal. The `connect` method is used to register `onSubjectChange` as an observer of `subject`. The `send` method is used to emit a signal with a value.
lessons.task: Implement a simple observer pattern using signals in Nim. Create a subject that emits a signal with a string message. Create a receiver that listens for this signal and prints the message. The receiver should be a procedure that gets called whenever the subject emits a signal. The expected output should be: `Received message: Hello, Observer Pattern!`.
lessons.solution:
import signals

var subject = newSubject[string]()

proc onMessageReceived(message: string) =
  echo "Received message: ", message

subject.connect(onMessageReceived)

subject.send("Hello, Observer Pattern!")
lessons.hint: Remember, the key is to connect the receiver to the subject and ensure the receiver gets called when the subject sends a signal. Don't overthink it, just follow the pattern.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, this can be achieved using the Observer pattern with classes and methods. In Rust, similar functionality can be implemented using traits and closures. In C++, this pattern is often realized using the Observer design pattern with classes and interfaces.

lessons.name: RAII with defer/destroy
lessons.concept: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) is a programming idiom used to manage resources. In Nim, you can use the `defer` and `destroy` statements to ensure that resources are properly cleaned up when they go out of scope. This prevents memory leaks and other resource management issues. For example, if you allocate memory with `alloc`, you should use `dealloc` to free it. The `defer` statement ensures that `dealloc` is called when the scope is exited, even if an error occurs. Similarly, `destroy` can be used to clean up objects that require explicit destruction.
lessons.task: Write a Nim program that allocates memory for a tuple containing an integer and a string. Use `defer` to ensure that the allocated memory is deallocated when the scope is exited. Print a message before and after the allocation to verify the order of operations.
lessons.solution:
import std/os

type
  Data = tuple[x: int, s: string]

proc main() =
  var d = cast[ptr Data](alloc0(sizeof(Data)))
  d.x = 42
  d.s = "hello"
  defer:
    dealloc(d)
    echo "Memory deallocated"
  echo "Memory allocated"

main()
lessons.hint: Remember, `defer` ensures that the block of code after it is executed when the scope is exited. This is perfect for cleaning up resources like allocated memory.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Using context managers with `with` statement / Rust equivalent: Using `Drop` trait / C++ equivalent: Using RAII with destructors

lessons.name: Dependency Injection
lessons.concept:
Dependency injection is a design pattern where you pass dependencies to a class or function instead of having it create them. This makes the code more flexible and easier to test. For example, instead of a class creating its own database connection, it receives a connection object. This way, you can inject different types of connections for different scenarios, like using a mock connection in tests.

Here's a simple example in Nim:
```nim
# Define an interface for a service
interface IService
  proc doSomething(self: IService) {.abstractmethod.}

# Implement the service
type
  ServiceImpl = object
  
proc doSomething(self: ServiceImpl) =
  echo "Doing something"

# Define a class that uses the service
type
  Consumer = object
    service: IService
  
proc useService(self: Consumer) =
  self.service.doSomething()

# Main function to demonstrate dependency injection
proc main() =
  let service = ServiceImpl()
  let consumer = Consumer(service: service)
  consumer.useService()

main()
```
In this example, `ServiceImpl` is a concrete implementation of the `IService` interface. The `Consumer` class uses an `IService` object, which is injected when the `Consumer` is created. This makes it easy to swap out the service implementation without changing the `Consumer` class.
lessons.task: Implement a simple dependency injection pattern in Nim. Create an interface `IMessageService` with a method `sendMessage`. Implement this interface in a class `EmailService` that prints "Sending email" when `sendMessage` is called. Create another class `Consumer` that uses an `IMessageService`. Inject an instance of `EmailService` into `Consumer` and call `sendMessage` on it. The output should be "Sending email".
lessons.solution:
# Define an interface for a message service
interface IMessageService
  proc sendMessage(self: IMessageService) {.abstractmethod.}

# Implement the message service
type
  EmailService = object
  
proc sendMessage(self: EmailService) =
  echo "Sending email"

# Define a class that uses the message service
type
  Consumer = object
    messageService: IMessageService
  
proc useService(self: Consumer) =
  self.messageService.sendMessage()

# Main function to demonstrate dependency injection
proc main() =
  let service = EmailService()
  let consumer = Consumer(messageService: service)
  consumer.useService()

main()
lessons.hint: Remember, dependency injection is about passing dependencies to classes instead of having them create them. In this case, you need to define an interface, implement it, and then inject it into the class that uses it.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Use of classes and method overriding. Rust equivalent: Use of traits and generics. C++ equivalent: Use of interfaces and virtual functions.

lessons.name: Protocol Design
lessons.concept:
In this lesson, we'll dive into designing a simple chat server using Nim's `async` and `await` features. This setup will allow the server to handle multiple clients simultaneously and broadcast messages to all connected clients. The `asyncdispatch` module is your go-to for asynchronous programming in Nim, providing the necessary tools to manage multiple connections efficiently.

Here's a quick rundown of what you'll need:
- `asyncdispatch` module for asynchronous operations.
- `accept` to handle incoming client connections.
- `recv` and `send` to manage data transmission.

Let's get started with a basic example. Imagine you have a server that listens on a port and accepts clients. When a client sends a message, the server broadcasts it to all connected clients.

```nim
import asyncdispatch

proc handleClient(client: AsyncFD) {.async.} =
  while true:
    let msg = await recv(client, 1024)
    if msg.len == 0: break
    for c in clients:
      if c != client:
        await send(c, msg)

var clients: seq[AsyncFD] = @[]

proc startServer(port: Port) {.async.} =
  let server = await createAsyncNativeSocket()
  await bindAddr(server, "0.0.0.0", port)
  await listen(server)
  while true:
    let client = await accept(server)
    clients.add(client)
    spawn handleClient(client)

waitFor startServer(12345)
```

In this snippet, `handleClient` is an asynchronous procedure that reads messages from a client and broadcasts them to all other clients. `startServer` sets up the server to listen on a specified port and spawn a new task for each client connection.

Remember, the key to handling multiple clients efficiently is to use `async` and `await` to manage asynchronous operations without blocking the main thread. This allows your server to handle multiple clients concurrently.

Now, it's your turn to implement this chat server. Good luck!
lessons.task:
Your task is to design and implement a simple chat server using Nim's `async` and `await` features. The server should be able to handle multiple clients simultaneously and broadcast messages to all connected clients. Your solution should include the following:

- Import the necessary modules.
- Define a procedure to handle client connections and message broadcasting.
- Set up the server to listen on a specified port and accept incoming connections.
- Spawn a new task for each client connection.

Expected output: The server should run indefinitely, accepting new connections and broadcasting messages to all clients. You can test the server by connecting multiple clients (e.g., using telnet or a custom client) and sending messages.

Filename: solution.nim
Type: single_file
Cmd: c
Skip_run: false
lessons.hint: Remember, the magic happens with `async` and `await`. Don't forget to spawn a new task for each client to handle them concurrently. Broadcasting messages is as simple as iterating over the list of clients and sending the message to each one, except the sender itself.

lessons.name: Server Implementation
lessons.concept:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to build a simple asynchronous chat server using Nim's `asyncdispatch` library. This server will handle multiple clients simultaneously and broadcast messages to all connected clients. The `asyncdispatch` library is a powerful tool for non-blocking IO operations, making it perfect for building scalable network applications. Here's a quick example of how you might set up a basic server:

```nim
import asyncdispatch

proc handleClient(client: AsyncFD) {.async.} =
  while true:
    let msg = await client.recv(1024)
    if msg.len == 0: break
    echo "Received: " & msg
    await client.send(msg)

waitFor asyncEchoServer(1234, handleClient)
```

In this snippet, `handleClient` is an asynchronous procedure that reads messages from a client and sends them back. The `asyncEchoServer` function sets up the server to listen on port 1234 and handle each client connection with `handleClient`. This is a basic example, but it illustrates the core concepts you'll be working with in this lesson.
lessons.task:
Your task is to implement a simple asynchronous chat server using Nim's `asyncdispatch` library. The server should be able to handle multiple clients simultaneously and broadcast messages to all connected clients. Here's what you need to do:

1. Import the necessary modules from the `asyncdispatch` library.
2. Define a procedure to handle client connections. This procedure should read messages from the client and broadcast them to all other connected clients.
3. Set up the server to listen on a specific port and handle incoming connections using the procedure you defined.

Your solution should be a complete Nim program that can be compiled and run. The server should be able to handle multiple clients and broadcast messages as described.
lessons.solution:
import asyncdispatch

var clients: seq[AsyncFD] = @[]

proc broadcast(msg: string) {.async.} =
  for client in clients:
    await client.send(msg)

proc handleClient(client: AsyncFD) {.async.} =
  clients.add(client)
  while true:
    let msg = await client.recv(1024)
    if msg.len == 0: break
    echo "Received: " & msg
    await broadcast(msg)
  clients.remove(client)

waitFor asyncEchoServer(1234, handleClient)
lessons.hint: Remember, the key to handling multiple clients simultaneously is to use asynchronous procedures. Each client should be handled in its own asynchronous procedure, allowing the server to manage multiple clients at the same time. Also, don't forget to broadcast messages to all connected clients, not just the one that sent the message.

lessons.name: Plugin API Basics
lessons.concept:
Macros in Nim are like magic spells that transform your code at compile time. They're powerful but can also be a bit of a headache if you're not careful. In this lesson, you'll create a macro that modifies a `for` loop to print each item prefixed with its index. It's like giving your loops a superpower. Here's a quick example of what you're aiming for:

```nim
for item in enumerate([1, 2, 3]):
  echo item
```

This should output:

```
0: 1
1: 2
2: 3
```

Got it? Good. Let's dive in.
lessons.task:
Write a Nim macro named `enumerate` that modifies a `for` loop to print each item prefixed with its index. The macro should work with any iterable. The expected output for the provided test code should be:

```
0: Hello
1: World
2: Nim
```

Make sure your macro is complete and compilable. The test code will check if the output is correct.
lessons.solution:
import std/macros

macro enumerate(x: ForLoopStmt): untyped =
  expectKind x, nnkForStmt
  var countStart = if x[^2].len == 2: newLit(0) else: x[^2][1]
  result = newStmtList()
  result.add newVarStmt(x[0], countStart)
  var body = x[^1]
  if body.kind != nnkStmtList:
    body = newTree(nnkStmtList, body)
  body.add newCall(bindSym"inc", x[0])
  var newFor = newTree(nnkForStmt)
  for i in 1..x.len-3:
    newFor.add x[i]
  newFor.add x[^2][^1]
  newFor.add body
  result.add newFor
  result = quote do:
    block: `result`

for item in enumerate(["Hello", "World", "Nim"]):
  echo item

lessons.name: Implementing a Linter
lessons.concept:
In this lesson, we'll dive into creating a macro in Nim that takes a static integer argument and prints it. Macros in Nim are powerful tools that allow you to manipulate the syntax tree at compile time. The `static` keyword is used to pass values as values, not as syntax tree nodes, which makes them ideal for this task. For example, `macro foo(arg: static[int])` will take an integer constant as an argument and treat it like a normal parameter of type `int` inside the macro body. This is different from regular macro arguments, which are syntax tree nodes. Here's a quick example to illustrate the concept:

```nim
import std/macros

macro myMacro(arg: static[int]): untyped =
  echo arg # just an int (7), not `NimNode`

myMacro(1 + 2 * 3)
```

In this example, `myMacro` takes a static integer argument and prints it. The expression `1 + 2 * 3` is evaluated at compile time, and the result, `7`, is passed to the macro as a regular integer.
lessons.task: Your task is to create a macro called `printStaticInt` that takes a static integer argument and prints it. The macro should be defined in a file named `solution.nim`. When you call `printStaticInt(42)`, it should print `42` to the console.
lessons.solution:
import std/macros

macro printStaticInt(arg: static[int]): untyped =
  echo arg

printStaticInt(42)
lessons.hint: Remember, the key here is to use the `static` keyword to ensure the argument is evaluated at compile time and passed as a regular integer. Don't try to treat it as a syntax tree node; just use it like any other integer.
lessons.cross_language_notes: In Python, similar functionality can be achieved using decorators with static arguments. In Rust, macros can also take static arguments, but the syntax and usage are different. In C++, template metaprogramming can be used to achieve similar static argument handling.

lessons.name: Property-Based Testing
lessons.concept:
Property-based testing is a technique where you test your code against a set of properties instead of specific examples. This approach helps ensure that your code behaves correctly for a wide range of inputs. In Nim, you can use the `hypothesis` library to implement property-based testing. Here's a quick example of how you might use it to test a sorting function:

```nim
import hypothesis

# Define a property that checks if a sorted list is in non-decreasing order
property sortedListIsNonDecreasing(list: seq[int]): bool =
  for i in 0..high(list - 1):
    if list[i] > list[i + 1]:
      return false
  return true

# Use hypothesis to test the property
hypothesis.test(sortedListIsNonDecreasing)
```

In this example, `sortedListIsNonDecreasing` is a property that checks if a list is sorted in non-decreasing order. The `hypothesis.test` function generates random lists and checks if the property holds for each one.
lessons.task: Implement a function that uses property-based testing to validate the correctness of a sorting algorithm. Your function should pass all generated test cases without errors. The sorting algorithm you will test is the built-in `sort` function in Nim.
lessons.hint: Remember, the key is to define a property that captures the essence of what it means for your sorting function to be correct. In this case, it's that the sorted list should be in non-decreasing order.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python equivalent: Use the `hypothesis` library for property-based testing. Rust equivalent: Use the `proptest` crate. C++ equivalent: Use the `catch2` framework with custom property-based testing.

lessons.name: nimpretty Formatter
lessons.concept:
The `nimpretty` formatter is your go-to tool for keeping your Nim code looking neat and tidy. It automatically formats your code according to Nim's style guidelines, making it easier to read and maintain. Unlike some other languages where you might have to manually adjust indentation or spacing, `nimpretty` does it all for you. Just run `nimpretty` on your Nim file, and watch it transform into a beautifully formatted masterpiece. Here's a quick example:

```nim
proc myProc(x: int): int =
  if x > 0:
    return x + 1
  else:
    return x - 1
```

After running `nimpretty`, it might look like this:

```nim
proc myProc(x: int): int =
  if x > 0:
    return x + 1
  else:
    return x - 1
```

See the difference? `nimpretty` takes care of the formatting so you don't have to.

```nim
return a * b
else:
return a + b
```

```nim
proc complexProc(a: int, b: int): int =
  if a > b:
    return a * b
  else:
    return a + b
```

Make sure to run `nimpretty` on your code and save the result as `solution.nim`.

lessons.name: Memory Layout Optimizations
lessons.concept:
Memory layout optimizations in Nim can be achieved using the `align` and `packed` pragmas. The `align` pragma modifies the alignment requirement of a variable or object field, while the `packed` pragma ensures that the fields of an object are packed back-to-back in memory. This can be particularly useful for performance-critical applications or when interfacing with C code. Here's a quick example:

```nim
type
  sseType = object
    sseData {.align(16).}: array[4, float32]

  Data = object
    x: char
    cacheline {.align(128).}: array[128, char]

proc main() =
  echo "sizeof(Data) = ", sizeof(Data), " (1 byte + 127 bytes padding + 128-byte array)"
  echo "alignment of sseType is ", alignof(sseType)

main()
```

In this example, `sseType` is aligned to 16 bytes, and `Data` has an alignment of 128 bytes, with 127 bytes of padding to ensure proper alignment.
lessons.task: Create a Nim program that defines two types: `OptimizedStruct` and `PackedStruct`. `OptimizedStruct` should have fields of different types with specific alignment requirements, while `PackedStruct` should have the same fields but packed together. Print the size and alignment of each type. The expected output should match the sizes and alignments based on the use of the `align` and `packed` pragmas.
lessons.solution:
import std/[strformat, macros]

# Define OptimizedStruct with specific alignment requirements
type
  OptimizedStruct = object
    a: int8 {.align(1).}
    b: int32 {.align(4).}
    c: int64 {.align(8).}

# Define PackedStruct with packed fields
type
  PackedStruct {.packed.} = object
    a: int8
    b: int32
    c: int64

proc main() =
  echo fmt"sizeof(OptimizedStruct) = {sizeof(OptimizedStruct)}, alignof(OptimizedStruct) = {alignof(OptimizedStruct)}"
  echo fmt"sizeof(PackedStruct) = {sizeof(PackedStruct)}, alignof(PackedStruct) = {alignof(PackedStruct)}"

main()
lessons.hint: Remember, `align` specifies the alignment requirement, and `packed` removes any padding between fields. Check the sizes and alignments of your structs to ensure they match the expected values.
lessons.cross_language_notes: Python: No direct equivalent, but similar concepts can be achieved using ctypes or struct modules. Rust: Similar concepts can be achieved using `repr(align(N))` and `repr(packed)`. C++: Directly supported using `alignas` and `packed` attributes.
